system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

# Project Context

The following project context files have been loaded and should be followed:

## AGENTS.md

# Hermes Agent - Development Guide

Instructions for AI coding assistants and developers working on the hermes-agent codebase.

## Development Environment

```bash
# Prefer .venv; fall back to venv if that's what your checkout has.
source .venv/bin/activate   # or: source venv/bin/activate
```

`scripts/run_tests.sh` probes `.venv` first, then `venv`, then
`$HOME/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv` (for worktrees that share a venv with the
main checkout).

entry points you'll actually edit.

**User config:** `~/.hermes/config.yaml` (settings), `~/.hermes/.env` (API keys only).

## File Dependency Chain

```
tools/registry.py  (no deps — imported by all tool files)
       ↑
tools/*.py  (each calls registry.register() at import time)
       ↑
model_tools.py  (imports tools/registry + triggers tool discovery)
       ↑
run_agent.py, cli.py, batch_runner.py, environments/
```

## AIAgent Class (run_agent.py)

The real `AIAgent.__init__` takes ~60 parameters (credentials, routing, callbacks,
session context, budget, credential pool, etc.). The signature below is the
minimum subset you'll usually touch — read `run_agent.py` for the full list.

```python
class AIAgent:
    def __init__(self,
        base_url: str = None,
        api_key: str = None,
        provider: str = None,
        api_mode: str = None,              # "chat_completions" | "codex_responses" | ...
        model: str = "",                   # empty → resolved from config/provider later
        max_iterations: int = 90,          # tool-calling iterations (shared with subagents)
        enabled_toolsets: list = None,
        disabled_toolsets: list = None,
        quiet_mode: bool = False,
        save_trajectories: bool = False,
        platform: str = None,              # "cli", "telegram", etc.
        session_id: str = None,
        skip_context_files: bool = False,
        skip_memory: bool = False,
        credential_pool=None,
        # ... plus callbacks, thread/user/chat IDs, iteration_budget, fallback_model,
        # checkpoints config, prefill_messages, service_tier, reasoning_config, etc.
    ): ...

    def chat(self, message: str) -> str:
        """Simple interface — returns final response string."""

    def run_conversation(self, user_message: str, system_message: str = None,
                         conversation_history: list = None, task_id: str = None) -> dict:
        """Full interface — returns dict with final_response + messages."""
```

### Agent Loop

The core loop is inside `run_conversation()` — entirely synchronous, with
interrupt checks, budget tracking, and a one-turn grace call:

```python
while (api_call_count < self.max_iterations and self.iteration_budget.remaining > 0) \
        or self._budget_grace_call:
    if self._interrupt_requested: break
    response = client.chat.completions.create(model=model, messages=messages, tools=tool_schemas)
    if response.tool_calls:
        for tool_call in response.tool_calls:
            result = handle_function_call(tool_call.name, tool_call.args, task_id)
            messages.append(tool_result_message(result))
        api_call_count += 1
    else:
        return response.content
```

Messages follow OpenAI format: `{"role": "system/user/assistant/tool", ...}`.
Reasoning content is stored in `assistant_msg["reasoning"]`.

## CLI Architecture (cli.py)

- **Rich** for banner/panels, **prompt_toolkit** for input with autocomplete
- **KawaiiSpinner** (`agent/display.py`) — animated faces during API calls, `┊` activity feed for tool results
- `load_cli_config()` in cli.py merges hardcoded defaults + user config YAML
- **Skin engine** (`hermes_cli/skin_engine.py`) — data-driven CLI theming; initialized from `display.skin` config key at startup; skins customize banner colors, spinner faces/verbs/wings, tool prefix, response box, branding text
- `process_command()` is a method on `HermesCLI` — dispatches on canonical command name resolved via `resolve_command()` from the central registry
- Skill slash commands: `agent/skill_commands.py` scans `~/.hermes/skills/`, injects as **user message** (not system prompt) to preserve prompt caching

### Slash Command Registry (`hermes_cli/commands.py`)

All slash commands are defined in a central `COMMAND_REGISTRY` list of `CommandDef` objects. Every downstream consumer derives from this registry automatically:

- **CLI** — `process_command()` resolves aliases via `resolve_command()`, dispatches on canonical name
- **Gateway** — `GATEWAY_KNOWN_COMMANDS` frozenset for hook emission, `resolve_command()` for dispatch
- **Gateway help** — `gateway_help_lines()` generates `/help` output
- **Telegram** — `telegram_bot_commands()` generates the BotCommand menu
- **Slack** — `slack_subcommand_map()` generates `/hermes` subcommand routing
- **Autocomplete** — `COMMANDS` flat dict feeds `SlashCommandCompleter`
- **CLI help** — `COMMANDS_BY_CATEGORY` dict feeds `show_help()`

### Adding a Slash Command

1. Add a `CommandDef` entry to `COMMAND_REGISTRY` in `hermes_cli/commands.py`:
```python
CommandDef("mycommand", "Description of what it does", "Session",
           aliases=("mc",), args_hint="[arg]"),
```
2. Add handler in `HermesCLI.process_command()` in `cli.py`:
```python
elif canonical == "mycommand":
    self._handle_mycommand(cmd_original)
```
3. If the command is available in the gateway, add a handler in `gateway/run.py`:
```python
if canonical == "mycommand":
    return await self._handle_mycommand(event)
```
4. For persistent settings, use `save_config_value()` in `cli.py`

**CommandDef fields:**
- `name` — canonical name without slash (e.g. `"background"`)
- `description` — human-readable description
- `category` — one of `"Session"`, `"Configuration"`, `"Tools & Skills"`, `"Info"`, `"Exit"`
- `aliases` — tuple of alternative names (e.g. `("bg",)`)
- `args_hint` — argument placeholder shown in help (e.g. `"<prompt>"`, `"[name]"`)
- `cli_only` — only available in the interactive CLI
- `gateway_only` — only available in messaging platforms
- `gateway_config_gate` — config dotpath (e.g. `"display.tool_progress_command"`); when set on a `cli_only` command, the command becomes available in the gateway if the config value is truthy. `GATEWAY_KNOWN_COMMANDS` always includes config-gated commands so the gateway can dispatch them; help/menus only show them when the gate is open.

**Adding an alias** requires only adding it to the `aliases` tuple on the existing `CommandDef`. No other file changes needed — dispatch, help text, Telegram menu, Slack mapping, and autocomplete all update automatically.

## TUI Architecture (ui-tui + tui_gateway)

The TUI is a full replacement for the classic (prompt_toolkit) CLI, activated via `hermes --tui` or `HERMES_TUI=1`.

### Process Model

```
hermes --tui
  └─ Node (Ink)  ──stdio JSON-RPC──  Python (tui_gateway)
       │                                  └─ AIAgent + tools + sessions
       └─ renders transcript, composer, prompts, activity
```

TypeScript owns the screen. Python owns sessions, tools, model calls, and slash command logic.

### Transport

Newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdio. Requests from Ink, events from Python. See `tui_gateway/server.py` for the full method/event catalog.

### Key Surfaces

| Surface | Ink component | Gateway method |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Chat streaming | `app.tsx` + `messageLine.tsx` | `prompt.submit` → `message.delta/complete` |
| Tool activity | `thinking.tsx` | `tool.start/progress/complete` |
| Approvals | `prompts.tsx` | `approval.respond` ← `approval.request` |
| Clarify/sudo/secret | `prompts.tsx`, `maskedPrompt.tsx` | `clarify/sudo/secret.respond` |
| Session picker | `sessionPicker.tsx` | `session.list/resume` |
| Slash commands | Local handler + fallthrough | `slash.exec` → `_SlashWorker`, `command.dispatch` |
| Completions | `useCompletion` hook | `complete.slash`, `complete.path` |
| Theming | `theme.ts` + `branding.tsx` | `gateway.ready` with skin data |

### Slash Command Flow

1. Built-in client commands (`/help`, `/quit`, `/clear`, `/resume`, `/copy`, `/paste`, etc.) handled locally in `app.tsx`
2. Everything else → `slash.exec` (runs in persistent `_SlashWorker` subprocess) → `command.dispatch` fallback

### Dev Commands

```bash
cd ui-tui
npm install       # first time
npm run dev       # watch mode (rebuilds hermes-ink + tsx --watch)
npm start         # production
npm run build     # full build (hermes-ink + tsc)
npm run type-check # typecheck only (tsc --noEmit)
npm run lint      # eslint
npm run fmt       # prettier
npm test          # vitest
```

### TUI in the Dashboard (`hermes dashboard` → `/chat`)

The dashboard embeds the real `hermes --tui` — **not** a rewrite.  See `hermes_cli/pty_bridge.py` + the `@app.websocket("/api/pty")` endpoint in `hermes_cli/web_server.py`.

- Browser loads `web/src/pages/ChatPage.tsx`, which mounts xterm.js's `Terminal` with the WebGL renderer, `@xterm/addon-fit` for container-driven resize, and `@xterm/addon-unicode11` for modern wide-character widths.
- `/api/pty?token=…` upgrades to a WebSocket; auth uses the same ephemeral `_SESSION_TOKEN` as REST, via query param (browsers can't set `Authorization` on WS upgrade).
- The server spawns whatever `hermes --tui` would spawn, through `ptyprocess` (POSIX PTY — WSL works, native Windows does not).
- Frames: raw PTY bytes each direction; resize via `\x1b[RESIZE:<cols>;<rows>]` intercepted on the server and applied with `TIOCSWINSZ`.

**Do not re-implement the primary chat experience in React.** The main transcript, composer/input flow (including slash-command behavior), and PTY-backed terminal belong to the embedded `hermes --tui` — anything new you add to Ink shows up in the dashboard automatically. If you find yourself rebuilding the transcript or composer for the dashboard, stop and extend Ink instead.

**Structured React UI around the TUI is allowed when it is not a second chat surface.** Sidebar widgets, inspectors, summaries, status panels, and similar supporting views (e.g. `ChatSidebar`, `ModelPickerDialog`, `ToolCall`) are fine when they complement the embedded TUI rather than replacing the transcript / composer / terminal. Keep their state independent of the PTY child's session and surface their failures non-destructively so the terminal pane keeps working unimpaired.

## Adding New Tools

For most custom or local-only tools, do **not** edit Hermes core. Use the plugin
route instead: create `~/.hermes/plugins/<name>/plugin.yaml` and
`~/.hermes/plugins/<name>/__

Use the pattern from `tests/hermes_cli/test_profiles.py`:
```python
@pytest.fixture
def profile_env(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
    home = tmp_path / ".hermes"
    home.mkdir()
    monkeypatch.setattr(Path, "home", lambda: tmp_path)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HERMES_HOME", str(home))

```

---

## Testing

**ALWAYS use `scripts/run_tests.sh`** — do not call `pytest` directly. The script enforces
hermetic environment parity with CI (unset credential vars, TZ=UTC, LANG=C.UTF-8,
4 xdist workers matching GHA ubuntu-latest). Direct `pytest` on a 16+ core
developer machine with API keys set diverges from CI in ways that have caused
multiple "works locally, fails in CI" incidents (and the reverse).

```bash
scripts/run_tests.sh                                  # full suite, CI-parity
scripts/run_tests.sh tests/gateway/                   # one directory
scripts/run_tests.sh tests/agent/test_foo.py::test_x  # one test
scripts/run_tests.sh -v --tb=long                     # pass-through pytest flags
```

### Why the wrapper (and why the old "just call pytest" doesn't work)

Five real sources of local-vs-CI drift the script closes:

| | Without wrapper | With wrapper |
|---|---|---|
| Provider API keys | Whatever is in your env (auto-detects pool) | All `*_API_KEY`/`*_TOKEN`/etc. unset |
| HOME / `~/.hermes/` | Your real config+auth.json | Temp dir per test |
| Timezone | Local TZ (PDT etc.) | UTC |
| Locale | Whatever is set | C.UTF-8 |
| xdist workers | `-n auto` = all cores (20+ on a workstation) | `-n 4` matching CI |

`tests/conftest.py` also enforces points 1-4 as an autouse fixture so ANY pytest
invocation (including IDE integrations) gets hermetic behavior — but the wrapper
is belt-and-suspenders.

### Running without the wrapper (only if you must)

If you can't use the wrapper (e.g. on Windows or inside an IDE that shells
pytest directly), at minimum activate the venv and pass `-n 4`:

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate   # or: source venv/bin/activate
python -m pytest tests/ -q -n 4
```

Worker count above 4 will surface test-ordering flakes that CI never sees.

Always run the full suite before pushing changes.

### Don't write change-detector tests

A test is a **change-detector** if it fails whenever data that is **expected
to change** gets updated — model catalogs, config version numbers,
enumeration counts, hardcoded lists of provider models. These tests add no
behavioral coverage; they just guarantee that routine source updates break
CI and cost engineering time to "fix."

**Do not write:**

```python
# catalog snapshot — breaks every model release
assert "gemini-2.5-pro" in _PROVIDER_MODELS["gemini"]
assert "MiniMax-M2.7" in models

# config version literal — breaks every schema bump
assert DEFAULT_CONFIG["_config_version"] == 21

# enumeration count — breaks every time a skill/provider is added
assert len(_PROVIDER_MODELS["huggingface"]) == 8
```

**Do write:**

```python
# behavior: does the catalog plumbing work at all?
assert "gemini" in _PROVIDER_MODELS
assert len(_PROVIDER_MODELS["gemini"]) >= 1

# behavior: does migration bump the user's version to current latest?
assert raw["_config_version"] == DEFAULT_CONFIG["_config_version"]

# invariant: no plan-only model leaks into the legacy list
assert not (set(moonshot_models) & coding_plan_only_models)

# invariant: every model in the catalog has a context-length entry
for m in _PROVIDER_MODELS["huggingface"]:
    assert m.lower() in DEFAULT_CONTEXT_LENGTHS_LOWER
```

The rule: if the test reads like a snapshot of current data, delete it. If
it reads like a contract about how two pieces of data must relate, keep it.
When a PR adds a new provider/model and you want a test, make the test
assert the relationship (e.g. "catalog entries all have context lengths"),
not the specific names.

Reviewers should reject new change-detector tests; authors should convert
them into invariants before re-requesting review.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

On delete, pass `absorbed_into=<umbrella>` when you're merging this skill's content into another one, or `absorbed_into=""` when you're pruning it with no forwarding target. This lets the curator tell consolidation from pruning without guessing, so downstream consumers (cron jobs that reference the old skill name, etc.) get updated correctly. The target you name in `absorbed_into` must already exist — create/patch the umbrella first, then delete.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are protected from deletion only — skill_manage(action='delete') will refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Patches and edits go through on pinned skills so you can still improve them as pitfalls come up; pin only guards against irrecoverable loss.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

absorbed_into.description: For 'delete' only — declares intent so the curator can tell consolidation from pruning without guessing. Pass the umbrella skill name when this skill's content was merged into another (the target must already exist). Pass an empty string when the skill is truly stale and being pruned with no forwarding target. Omitting the arg on delete is supported for backward compatibility but downstream tooling (e.g. cron-job skill reference rewriting) will have to guess at intent.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

Guarda, il problema principale è chiarissimo:

**`-W` in ttyd 1.7.4 significa `--writable`**, non write-only! Quindi **non è un problema**. Anzi, senza `-W` il terminale sarebbe in readonly e non potresti scrivere. Quindi `-W` è corretto per un'app interattiva.

Ma ci sono altri problemi:

1. **Doppio annidamento `bash -c`** — il quoting è ok, ma non necessario
2. **Il comando `./hermes`** — presuppone che tu sia già in `/home/alfdib/Hermes/hermes-agent/` ma ttyd lancia il comando dal suo working directory (che è sconosciuto). Tuttavia il `cd` all'interno del bash -c annidato dovrebbe risolvere.
3. **Problema subdolo**: ttyd NON fa una shell login/interactive completa. Quando lancia `bash -c '...'`, non carica `.bashrc`/`.bash_profile`/`.profile`. Questo significa che:
   - Il PATH di WSL vanilla potrebbe non avere `/home/alfdib/Hermes/hermes-agent/.venv/bin`
   - `./hermes` è uno script che probabilmente attiva un virtualenv — se fallisce, il comando muore
   - Variabili d'ambiente come `HERMES_HOME` e altre non vengono settate

Fammi vedere che cos'è `./hermes` per capire meglio.

Ma soprattutto: **qual è il sintomo?** Non funziona proprio? Si connette ma non fa nulla? Errore?

È un design comune per CLI tool (come `git`, `gh`, `npm`) — la config è globale all'utente, non per progetto. Il `config.yaml` dentro `hermes-agent/` non verrebbe mai guardato.

Il `config.yaml` in `~/.hermes/` **c'è** (55 byte), ma è praticamente vuoto. Manca la configurazione del provider. Esempio di quello che ci vuole per farlo funzionare con OpenRouter:

     2|# Copy this file to .env and fill in your API keys
     3|
     4|# =============================================================================
     5|# LLM PROVIDER (OpenRouter)
     6|# =============================================================================
     7|# OpenRouter provides access to many models through one API
     8|# All LLM calls go through OpenRouter - no direct provider keys needed
     9|# Get your key at: https://openrouter.ai/keys
    10|# OPENROUTER_API_KEY=
    11|
    12|# Default model is configured in ~/.hermes/config.yaml (model.default).
    13|# Use 'hermes model' or 'hermes setup' to change it.
    14|# LLM_MODEL is no longer read from .env — this line is kept for reference only.
    15|# LLM_MODEL=anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
    16|
    17|# =============================================================================
    18|# LLM PROVIDER (Google AI Studio / Gemini)
    19|# =============================================================================
    20|# Native Gemini API via Google's OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
    21|# Get your key at: https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey
    22|# GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_google_ai_studio_key_here
    23|# GEMINI_API_KEY=your_gemini_key_here  # alias for GOOGLE_API_KEY
    24|# Optional base URL override (default: Google's OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
    25|# GEMINI_BASE_URL=https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai
    26|
    27|# =============================================================================
    28|# LLM PROVIDER (Ollama Cloud)
    29|# =============================================================================
    30|# Cloud-hosted open models via Ollama's OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

messages.content: come faccio a cambiare AI da qui? devo dare Model?

In Hermes, da dentro la chat interattiva, usi il comando slash:

```
/model <nome-modello>
```

Esempi:

```
/model deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
/model deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash
/model openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
/model openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528
```

Se vuoi vedere il modello corrente:

```
/model
```

Se vuoi vedere tutti i modelli disponibili (c'è anche come alias):

```
/help model
```

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "python3 -c "
import json
with open('/home/alfdib/Hermes/hermes-data/models_dev_cache.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

# Check if deepseek-v4-pro exists under deepseek provider
if 'deepseek' in data:
    print('=== DeepSeek provider models ===')
    models = data['deepseek'].get('models', {})
    for m in models:
        print(f'  - {m}')
print()

# Check if deepseek-v4-pro exists under openrouter provider
if 'openrouter' in data:
    print('=== OpenRouter provider (checking deepseek models) ===')
    models = data['openrouter'].get('models', {})
    deepseek_models = [m for m in models if 'v4' in m or 'deepseek' in m]
    for m in deepseek_models:
        print(f'  - {m}')
"", "timeout": 10}

messages.reasoning:
Now I see the problem clearly:

1. Both `deepseek-v4-flash` and `deepseek-v4-pro` exist in the **DeepSeek provider** (native API).
2. But `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` and `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` also exist in the **OpenRouter provider**.

When the user types `--model deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`, the format is `provider/model_name`. So:
- Provider = `deepseek`
- Model = `deepseek-v4-pro`

The provider `deepseek` is the native DeepSeek provider with `api: "https://api.deepseek.com"`. So in theory it should route to the native DeepSeek API.

But the user says it showed "Provider: OpenRouter" when switching. This means Hermes is interpreting the model name differently — perhaps it's using the `deepseek` prefix in the openrouter provider's model list, or there's some routing logic that decides to use OpenRouter for this model.

Let me think about this differently. When Hermes routes models, it might:
1. Parse the model name `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` as `provider=deepseek, model=deepseek-v4-pro`
2. The `deepseek` provider has `deepseek-v4-pro` in its model list
3. But if the user has some default provider setting, or if the `deepseek` provider can't authenticate (no API key), it falls back to OpenRouter

Wait, the user showed "Provider: OpenRouter" in the output. That means Hermes determined that `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` should be served via OpenRouter, not the native DeepSeek API.

This could happen if:
1. The model is found in both the DeepSeek provider AND the OpenRouter provider's model lists
2. Hermes has a preference/priority system that picks OpenRouter over DeepSeek

Or maybe the model name `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` is being parsed differently — since OpenRouter has models named `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`, the `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` model name could be matching as an OpenRouter model where:
- Provider = `openrouter`
- Model = `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`

In that case, the parsing is: take the first segment before `/` as the provider. But `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` has two slashes... no wait, it doesn't. It's `deepseek` (provider) / `deepseek-v4-pro` (model).

Actually, thinking about this differently — maybe the issue is that when Hermes resolves a model name, it checks if the model exists in a "friendly" or most common provider. Since `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` is available on OpenRouter, and OpenRouter is a common/default routing provider, Hermes might prefer OpenRouter.

The important thing is: the user wants `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` to go through the native DeepSeek API, not OpenRouter. To force this, they need to specify it in a way that avoids the OpenRouter routing. 

The solution is to configure the `deepseek` provider explicitly in config.yaml to be the default routing for deepseek models, or to set the default provider to deepseek.

Actually, let me look at this from a different angle. Let me check how Hermes resolves model-to-provider mappings.

model:

  6044|        """Handle /model command — switch model for this session.
  6045|
  6046|        Supports:

  6048|          /model <name>                       — switch for this session only
  6049|          /model <name> --global              — switch and persist to config.yaml

  6051|          /model --provider <provider>        — switch to provider, auto-detect model

  6054|        from hermes_cli.providers import get_label
  6055|
  6056|        # Parse args from the original command

  6058|        raw_args = parts[1].strip() if len(parts) > 1 else ""
  6059|
  6060|        # Parse --provider and --global flags

  6062|
  6063|        # Load providers for switch_model (picker path needs them below)

  6065|        custom_provs = None
  6066|        try:

  6068|            cfg = load_config()
  6069|            user_provs = cfg.get("providers")
  6070|            custom_provs = get_compatible_custom_providers(cfg)
  6071|        except Exception:
  6072|            pass
  6073|
  6074|        # No args at all: open prompt_toolkit-native picker modal
  6075|        if not model_input and not explicit_provider:
  6076|            model_display = self.model or "unknown"
  6077|            provider_display = get_label(self.provider) if self.provider else "unknown"
  6078|
  6079|            try:
  6080|                providers = list_authenticated_providers(
  6081|                    current_provider=self.provider or "",
  6082|                    current_base_url=self.base_url or "",
  6083|                    current_model=self.model or "",
  6084|                    user_providers=user_provs,
  6085|                    custom_providers=custom_provs,
  6086|                    max_models=50,
  6087|                )
  6088|            except Exception:
  6089|                providers = []
  6090|
  6091|            if not providers:
  6092|                _cprint("  No authenticated providers found.")
  6093|                _cprint("")
  6094|                _cprint("  /model <name>                        switch model")
  6095|                _cprint("  /model --provider <slug>             switch provider")
  6096|                return
  6097|
  6098|            self._open_model_picker(
  6099|                providers,
  6100|                model_display,
  6101|                provider_display,
  6102|                user_provs=user_provs,
  6103|                custom_provs=custom_provs,
  6104|            )
  6105|            return
  6106|
  6107|        # Perform the switch
  6108|        result = switch_model(
  6109|            raw_input=model_input,
  6110|            current_provider=self.provider or "",
  6111|            current_model=self.model or "",
  6112|            current_base_url=self.base_url or "",
  6113|            current_api_key=self.api_key or "",
  6114|            is_global=persist_global,
  6115|            explicit_provider=explicit_provider,
  6116|            user_providers=user_provs,
  6117|            custom_providers=custom_provs,
  6118|        )
  6119|
  6120|        if not result.success:
  6121|            _cprint(f"  ✗ {result.error_message}")
  6122|            return
  6123|
  6124|        # Apply to CLI state.
  6125|        # Update requested_provider so _ensure_runtime_credentials() doesn't
  6126|        # overwrite the switch on the next turn (it re-resolves from this).
  6127|        old_model = self.model
  6128|        self.model = result.new_model
  6129|        self.provider = result.target_provider
  6130|        self.requested_provider = result.target_provider
  6131|        # Always overwrite explicit overrides so stale credentials from the
  6132|        # previous provider (e.g. Ollama api_key/base_url) don't leak into
  6133|        # the new provider's credential resolution on the next turn.
  6134|        self._explicit_api_key = result.api_key
  6135|        self._explicit_base_url = result.base_url
  6136|        if result.api_key:
  6137|            self.api_key = result.api_key
  6138|        if result.base_url:
  6139|            self.base_url = result.base_url
  6140|        if result.api_mode:
  6141|            self.api_mode = result.api_mode

  6143|        # Apply to running agent (in-place swap)
  6144|        if self.agent is not None:
  6145|            try:
  6146|                self.agent.switch_model(
  6147|                    new_model=result.new_model,
  6148|                    new_provider=result.target_provider,
  6149|                    api_key=result.api_key,
  6150|                    base_url=result.base_url,
  6151|                    api_mode=result.api_mode,
  6152|                )
  6153|            except Exception as exc:
  6154|                _cprint(f"  ⚠ Agent swap failed ({exc}); change applied to next session.")
  6155|
  6156|        # Store a note to prepend to the next user message so the model
  6157|        # knows a switch occurred (avoids injecting system messages mid-history
  6158|        # which breaks providers and prompt caching).
  6159|        self._pending_model_switch_note = (

  6163|        )
  6164|
  6165|        # Display confirmation with full metadata
  6166|        provider_label = result.provider_label or result.target_provider
  6167|        _cprint(f"  ✓ Model switched: {result.new_model}")
  6168|        _cprint(f"    Provider: {provider_label}")
  6169|
  6170|        # Context: always resolve via the provider-aware chain so Codex OAuth,
  6171|        # Copilot, and Nous-enforced caps win over the raw models.dev entry
  6172|        # (e.g. gpt-5.5 is 1.05M on openai but 272K on Codex OAuth).
  6173|        mi = result.model_info
  6174|        from hermes_cli.model_switch import resolve_display_context_length
  6175|        ctx = resolve_display_context_length(
  6176|            result.new_model,
  6177|            result.target_provider,
  6178|            base_url=result.base_url or self.base_url or "",
  6179|            api_key=result.api_key or self.api_key or "",
  6180|            model_info=mi,
  6181|            config_context_length=getattr(self.agent, "_config_context_length", None) if self.agent else None,
  6182|        )
  6183|        if ctx:
  6184|            _cprint(f"    Context: {ctx:,} tokens")
  6185|        if mi:
  6186|            if mi.max_output:
  6187|                _cprint(f"    Max output: {mi.max_output:,} tokens")
  6188|            if mi.has_cost_data():
  6189|                _cprint(f"    Cost: {mi.format_cost()}")
  6190|            _cprint(f"    Capabilities: {mi.format_capabilities()}")
  6191|
  6192|        # Cache notice
  6193|        cache_enabled = (
  6194|            (base_url_host_matches(result.base_url or "", "openrouter.ai") and "claude" in result.new_model.lower())
  6195|            or result.api_mode == "anthropic_messages"
  6196|        )
  6197|        if cache_enabled:
  6198|            _cprint("    Prompt caching: enabled")
  6199|
  6200|        # Warning from validation
  6201|        if result.warning_message:
  6202|            _cprint(f"    ⚠ {result.warning_message}")
  6203|
  6204|        # Persistence
  6205|        if persist_global:
  6206|            save_config_value("model.default", result.new_model)
  6207|            if result.provider_changed:
  6208|                save_config_value("model.provider", result.target_provider)
  6209|            _cprint("    Saved to config.yaml (--global)")
  6210|        else:
  6211|            _cprint("    (session only — add --global to persist)")

  6214|        """Return True when /model should be handled immediately on the UI thread."""
  6215|        if not text or has_images or not _looks_like_slash_command(text):

{"content": "   615|def switch_model(
   616|    raw_input: str,
   617|    current_provider: str,
   618|    current_model: str,
   619|    current_base_url: str = "",
   620|    current_api_key: str = "",
   621|    is_global: bool = False,
   622|    explicit_provider: str = "",
   623|    user_providers: dict = None,
   624|    custom_providers: list | None = None,
   625|) -> ModelSwitchResult:
   626|    """Core model-switching pipeline shared between CLI and gateway.
   627|
   628|    Resolution chain:
   629|
   630|      If --provider given:
   631|        a. Resolve provider via resolve_provider_full()
   632|        b. Resolve credentials
   633|        c. If model given, resolve alias on target provider or use as-is
   634|        d. If no model, auto-detect from endpoint
   635|
   636|      If no --provider:
   637|        a. Try alias resolution on current provider
   638|        b. If alias exists but not on current provider -> fallback
   639|        c. On aggregator, try vendor/model slug conversion
   640|        d. Aggregator catalog search
   641|        e. detect_provider_for_model() as last resort
   642|        f. Resolve credentials
   643|        g. Normalize model name for target provider
   644|
   645|      Finally:
   646|        h. Get full model metadata from models.dev
   647|        i. Build result
   648|
   649|    Args:
   650|        raw_input: The model name (after flag parsing).
   651|        current_provider: The currently active provider.
   652|        current_model: The currently active model name.
   653|        current_base_url: The currently active base URL.
   654|        current_api_key: The currently active API key.
   655|        is_global: Whether to persist the switch.
   656|        explicit_provider: From --provider flag (empty = no explicit provider).
   657|        user_providers: The ``providers:`` dict from config.yaml (for user endpoints).
   658|        custom_providers: The ``custom_providers:`` list from config.yaml.
   659|
   660|    Returns:
   661|        ModelSwitchResult with all information the caller needs.
   662|    """
   663|    from hermes_cli.models import (
   664|        copilot_model_api_mode,
   665|        detect_provider_for_model,
   666|        validate_requested_model,
   667|        opencode_model_api_mode,
   668|    )
   669|    from hermes_cli.runtime_provider import resolve_runtime_provider
   670|
   671|    resolved_alias = ""
   672|    new_model = raw_input.strip()
   673|    target_provider = current_provider
   674|
   675|    # =================================================================
   676|    # PATH A: Explicit --provider given
   677|    # =================================================================
   678|    if explicit_provider:
   679|        # Resolve the provider
   680|        pdef = resolve_provider_full(
   681|            explicit_provider,
   682|            user_providers,
   683|            custom_providers,
   684|        )
   685|        if pdef is None:
   686|            _switch_err = (
   687|                f"Unknown provider '{explicit_provider}'. "
   688|                f"Check 'hermes model' for available providers, or define it "
   689|                f"in config.yaml under 'providers:'."
   690|            )
   691|            # Check for common config issues that cause provider resolution failures
   692|            try:
   693|                from hermes_cli.config import validate_config_structure
   694|                _cfg_issues = validate_config_structure()
   695|                if _cfg_issues:
   696|                    _switch_err += "\n\nRun 'hermes doctor' — config issues detected:"
   697|                    for _ci in _cfg_issues[:3]:
   698|                        _switch_err += f"\n  • {_ci.message}"
   699|            except Exception:
   700|                pass
   701|            return ModelSwitchResult(
   702|                success=False,
   703|                is_global=is_global,
   704|                error_message=_switch_err,
   705|            )
   706|
   707|        target_provider = pdef.id
   708|
   709|        # If no model specified, try auto-detect from endpoint
   710|        if not new_model:
   711|            if pdef.base_url:
   712|                from hermes_cli.runtime_provider import _auto_detect_local_model

This means Hermes routes `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` through OpenRouter instead of the native DeepSeek API!

The solution is to use native DeepSeek provider models differently. When you want to force native DeepSeek, you need to either:

2. Or add explicit provider configuration to config.yaml

{"content": "  1588|def detect_static_provider_for_model(
  1589|    model_name: str,
  1590|    current_provider: str,
  1591|) -> Optional[tuple[str, str]]:
  1592|    """Auto-detect a provider from static catalogs only.
  1593|
  1594|    Returns ``(provider_id, model_name)``. The model name may be remapped
  1595|    when a static alias or bare provider name resolves to a catalog default.
  1596|    Returns ``None`` when no confident match is found.
  1597|    """
  1598|    name = (model_name or "").strip()
  1599|    if not name:
  1600|        return None
  1601|
  1602|    name_lower = name.lower()
  1603|    current_keys = _provider_keys(current_provider)
  1604|
  1605|    alias_match = _resolve_static_model_alias(name_lower, current_keys)
  1606|    if alias_match:
  1607|        return alias_match
  1608|
  1609|    # --- Step 0: bare provider name typed as model ---
  1610|    # If someone types `/model nous` or `/model anthropic`, treat it as a
  1611|    # provider switch and pick the first model from that provider's catalog.
  1612|    # Skip "custom" and "openrouter" — custom has no model catalog, and
  1613|    # openrouter requires an explicit model name to be useful.
  1614|    resolved_provider = _PROVIDER_ALIASES.get(name_lower, name_lower)
  1615|    if resolved_provider not in {"custom", "openrouter"}:
  1616|        default_models = _PROVIDER_MODELS.get(resolved_provider, [])
  1617|        if (
  1618|            resolved_provider in _PROVIDER_LABELS
  1619|            and default_models
  1620|            and resolved_provider not in current_keys
  1621|        ):
  1622|            return (resolved_provider, default_models[0])
  1623|
  1624|    # Aggregators list other providers' models — never auto-switch TO them
  1625|    # If the model belongs to the current provider's catalog, don't suggest switching
  1626|    if _model_in_provider_catalog(name_lower, current_keys):
  1627|        return None
  1628|
  1629|    # --- Step 1: check static provider catalogs for a direct match ---
  1630|    for pid, models in _PROVIDER_MODELS.items():
  1631|        if pid in current_keys or pid in _AGGREGATOR_PROVIDERS:
  1632|            continue
  1633|        if any(name_lower == m.lower() for m in models):
  1634|            return (pid, name)
  1635|
  1636|    return None

   425|                        defaults["model"]["default"] = file_config["model"]["model"]

   427|            # Legacy root-level provider/base_url fallback.

   429|            # config root instead of inside the model: section.  These are
   430|            # only used as a FALLBACK when model.provider / model.base_url
   431|            # is not already set — never as an override.  The canonical
   432|            # location is model.provider (written by `hermes model`).
   433|            if not defaults["model"].get("provider"):
   434|                root_provider = file_config.get("provider")
   435|                if root_provider:
   436|                    defaults["model"]["provider"] = root_provider

Capito il problema. Hermes vede `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` e lo matcha esattamente col catalogo di **OpenRouter** (che ha il modello come `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`). Il provider nativo DeepSeek invece lo chiama `deepseek-v4-pro` (senza prefisso). Quindi Hermes pensa "questo è un modello OpenRouter" e ti fa passare da lì.

**Soluzione:** forzare il provider esplicitamente con `--provider deepseek`.

Nel comando ttyd (V4 Pro e V4 Flash):

```
wsl.exe -d Ubuntu bash -c "HERMES_HOME=/home/alfdib/Hermes/hermes-data /usr/bin/ttyd -p 7681 -W bash -c 'cd /home/alfdib/Hermes/hermes-agent && ./hermes chat --model deepseek-v4-pro --provider deepseek'"
```

```
wsl.exe -d Ubuntu bash -c "HERMES_HOME=/home/alfdib/Hermes/hermes-data /usr/bin/ttyd -p 7682 -W bash -c 'cd /home/alfdib/Hermes/hermes-agent && ./hermes chat --model deepseek-v4-flash --provider deepseek'"
```

Così Hermes sa che deve usare l'API `https://api.deepseek.com` con `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`.

Per gli altri due (Kimi e R1-0528) invece lascia `openrouter/...` — quelli funzionano già correttamente via OpenRouter senza modifiche.

2. The native DeepSeek provider's catalog lists it as `deepseek-v4-pro` (without the `deepseek/` prefix)

- `--model openrouter/...` → va su OpenRouter (per Kimi, R1)

# AGENTS.md - Your Workspace

This folder is home. Treat it that way.

## First Run

If `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists, that's your birth certificate. Follow it, figure out who you are, then delete it. You won't need it again.

## Session Startup

Before doing anything else:

1. Read `SOUL.md` — this is who you are
2. Read `USER.md` — this is who you're helping
3. Read `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` (today + yesterday) for recent context
4. **If in MAIN SESSION** (direct chat with your human): Also read `MEMORY.md`

Don't ask permission. Just do it.

## Memory

You wake up fresh each session. These files are your continuity:

- **Daily notes:** `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` (create `memory/` if needed) — raw logs of what happened
- **Long-term:** `MEMORY.md` — your curated memories, like a human's long-term memory

Capture what matters. Decisions, context, things to remember. Skip the secrets unless asked to keep them.

### 🧠 MEMORY.md - Your Long-Term Memory

- **ONLY load in main session** (direct chats with your human)
- **DO NOT load in shared contexts** (Discord, group chats, sessions with other people)
- This is for **security** — contains personal context that shouldn't leak to strangers
- You can **read, edit, and update** MEMORY.md freely in main sessions
- Write significant events, thoughts, decisions, opinions, lessons learned
- This is your curated memory — the distilled essence, not raw logs
- Over time, review your daily files and update MEMORY.md with what's worth keeping

### 📝 Write It Down - No "Mental Notes"!

- **Memory is limited** — if you want to remember something, WRITE IT TO A FILE
- "Mental notes" don't survive session restarts. Files do.
- When someone says "remember this" → update `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` or relevant file
- When you learn a lesson → update AGENTS.md, TOOLS.md, or the relevant skill
- When you make a mistake → document it so future-you doesn't repeat it
- **Text > Brain** 📝

## Writing Rules for Obsidian

**These rules apply to EVERY file written to `/home/alfdib/ObsidianVault/`.**

### Frontmatter (Every New File)
Every new file MUST have YAML frontmatter:
```yaml
---
type: log | entity | concept | project | source
created: YYYY-MM-DD
tags: [relevant, tags]
---
```

### Wikilink Policy
**ALWAYS use [[wikilinks]] when writing to Obsidian vault files.**

1. **Entities** (people, companies, projects):
   - First mention: `[[EntityName]]`
   - Create entity file if missing:
     - Companies: `entities/companies/CompanyName.md`
     - People: `entities/people/PersonName.md`
     - Projects: `entities/projects/ProjectName.md`
   - Example: "Met with [[John Rossi]] about [[TSMC]] expansion"

2. **Concepts** (frameworks, methodologies):
   - Link to existing concept files: `concepts/ConceptName.md`
   - Example: "Applied [[Porter Five Forces]] analysis"

3. **Cross-references**:
   - When citing a source: `[[YYYY-MM-DD_source_name.md]]`
   - When mentioning another project: `[[ProjectName]]`

4. **DO NOT wikilink** in: code blocks, URLs, or YAML frontmatter.

### "Related" Section
Every file MUST end with a cross-reference section:
```markdown
## Related
- [[RelatedPage1]]
- [[RelatedPage2]]
```

### Examples

**Good ✅**
```markdown
---
type: log
created: 2026-04-25
tags: [microe, sales, report]
---

# MicroE Report Week 17

[[TSMC]] announced 2nm fab expansion in Arizona.
Discussed with [[John Rossi]] using [[Competitive Framework]].

Sources: [[2026-04-25_tsmc_news.md]]

## Related
- [[Weekly Report 2026-04-18]]
- [[Data Center Project]]
```

**Bad ❌**
```markdown
# MicroE Report Week 17

TSMC announced 2nm fab expansion in Arizona.
Discussed with John Rossi using competitive analysis.
```

**ENFORCE THIS IN EVERY FILE YOU WRITE TO THE OBSIDIAN VAULT.**

## Red Lines

- Don't exfiltrate private data. Ever.
- Don't run destructive commands without asking.
- `trash` > `rm` (recoverable beats gone forever)
- When in doubt, ask.

## External vs Internal

**Safe to do freely:**

- Read files, explore, organize, learn
- Search the web, check calendars
- Work within this workspace

**Ask first:**

- Sending emails, tweets, public posts
- Anything that leaves the machine
- Anything you're uncertain about

## Group Chats

You have access to your human's stuff. That doesn't mean you _share_ their stuff. In groups, you're a participant — not their voice, not their proxy. Think before you speak.

### 💬 Know When to Speak!

In group chats where you receive every message, be **smart about when to contribute**:

**Respond when:**

- Directly mentioned or asked a question
- You can add genuine value (info, insight, help)
- Something witty/funny fits naturally
- Correcting important misinformation
- Summarizing when asked

**Stay silent (HEARTBEAT_OK) when:**

- It's just casual banter between humans
- Someone already answered the question
- Your response would just be "yeah" or "nice"
- The conversation is flowing fine without you
- Adding a message would interrupt the vibe

**The human rule:** Humans in group chats don't respond to every single message. Neither should you. Quality > quantity. If you wouldn't send it in a real group chat with friends, don't send it.

**Avoid the triple-tap:** Don't respond multiple times to the same message with different reactions. One thoughtful response beats three fragments.

Participate, don't dominate.

### 😊 React Like a Human!

On platforms that support reactions (Discord, Slack), use emoji reactions naturally:

**React when:**

- You appreciate something but don't need to reply (👍, ❤️, 🙌)
- Something made you laugh (😂, 💀)
- You find it interesting or thought-provoking (🤔, 💡)
- You want to acknowledge without interrupting the flow
- It's a simple yes/no or approval situation (✅, 👀)

**Why it matters:**
Reactions are lightweight social signals. Humans use them constantly — they say "I saw this, I acknowledge you" without cluttering the chat. You should too.

**Don't overdo it:** One reaction per message max. Pick the one that fits best.

## Tools

Skills provide your tools. When you need one, check its `SKILL.md`. Keep local notes (camera names, SSH details, voice preferences) in `TOOLS.md`.

**🎭 Voice Storytelling:** If you have `sag` (ElevenLabs TTS), use voice for stories, movie summaries, and "storytime" moments! Way more engaging than walls of text. Surprise people with funny voices.

**📝 Platform Formatting:**

- **Discord/WhatsApp:** No markdown tables! Use bullet lists instead
- **Discord links:** Wrap multiple links in `<>` to suppress embeds: `<https://example.com>`
- **WhatsApp:** No headers — use **bold** or CAPS for emphasis

## 💓 Heartbeats - Be Proactive!

When you receive a heartbeat poll (message matches the configured heartbeat prompt), don't just reply `HEARTBEAT_OK` every time. Use heartbeats productively!

Default heartbeat prompt:
`Read HEARTBEAT.md if it exists (workspace context). Follow it strictly. Do not infer or repeat old tasks from prior chats. If nothing needs attention, reply HEARTBEAT_OK.`

You are free to edit `HEARTBEAT.md` with a short checklist or reminders. Keep it small to limit token burn.

### Heartbeat vs Cron: When to Use Each

**Use heartbeat when:**

- Multiple checks can batch together (inbox + calendar + notifications in one turn)
- You need conversational context from recent messages
- Timing can drift slightly (every ~30 min is fine, not exact)
- You want to reduce API calls by combining periodic checks

**Use cron when:**

- Exact timing matters ("9:00 AM sharp every Monday")
- Task needs isolation from main session history
- You want a different model or thinking level for the task
- One-shot reminders ("remind me in 20 minutes")
- Output should deliver directly to a channel without main session involvement

**Tip:** Batch similar periodic checks into `HEARTBEAT.md` instead of creating multiple cron jobs. Use cron for precise schedules and standalone tasks.

**Things to check (rotate through these, 2-4 times per day):**

- **Emails** - Any urgent unread messag

   192|    def build_pivot_with_totals(source_df, row_key, col_key):

   206|            .reset_index()

   208|        agg['rank'] = agg.groupby('BU')['EUR'].rank(method='first', ascending=False)
   209|        top = agg[agg['rank'] <= top_n].copy()
   210|        pivot = top.pivot(index=row_key, columns='BU', values='EUR').fillna(0)
   211|        bu_order = ['CSM', 'EPP', 'HQ', 'MWIS', 'T&P']
   212|        for bu in bu_order:

   247|        total_df = pd.DataFrame([total_row])

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
filter_microe_dc.py — Sales report filter for Microelectronics & Data Center.

Legge due report Excel (WTS da SFDC1, VWT da SFDC2), li standardizza usando
le configurazioni in schema_config.yaml, filtra per segmento MicroE/DC, e
genera un report Excel riepilogativo con pivot e top-10.

CONFIG-DRIVEN: tutte le mappature colonne, pattern, override e regole sono
in schema_config.yaml. Quando il team finance cambia formato, modifica solo
lo YAML, non questo file Python.
"""

import datetime
import os
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import yaml
from openpyxl.utils import get_column_letter
from openpyxl.worksheet.table import Table, TableStyleInfo

# ─────────────────────────── PATHS ───────────────────────────

SCRIPT_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.resolve()
DATA_DIR = SCRIPT_DIR / "data"
SOURCE_DIR = SCRIPT_DIR / "source"
CONFIG_PATH = SCRIPT_DIR / "schema_config.yaml"
DATA_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
SOURCE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# ─────────────────────────── CONFIG LOADER ───────────────────

def load_config(path: Path = CONFIG_PATH) -> dict:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f)


# ─────────────────────────── FUZZY COLUMN MATCHING ───────────

def _tokenize(s: str) -> set:
    """Split a string into lowercase tokens for overlap matching."""
    return set(re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", s.lower()))


def _column_score(col_name: str, alias: str) -> float:
    """
    Score how well col_name matches an alias (0.0 = no match, 1.0 = exact).
    Uses token overlap / Jaccard similarity.
    """
    a_tokens = _tokenize(col_name)
    b_tokens = _tokenize(alias)
    if not a_tokens or not b_tokens:
        return 1.0 if col_name.lower() == alias.lower() else 0.0
    intersection = a_tokens & b_tokens
    union = a_tokens | b_tokens
    return len(intersection) / len(union)


def find_column(df: pd.DataFrame, aliases: list[str], threshold: float = 0.6) -> str | None:
    """
    Trova la colonna migliore nel DataFrame data una lista di alias.
    Usa fuzzy matching case-insensitive con token overlap.
    threshold: punteggio minimo (0-1) per considerare un match.
    """
    best_score = 0.0
    best_col = None
    for col in df.columns:
        col_str = str(col)
        for alias in aliases:
            # Exact match (case-insensitive) → immediate win
            if col_str.lower().strip() == alias.lower().strip():
                return col_str
            score = _column_score(col_str, alias)
            if score > best_score:
                best_score = score
                best_col = col_str
    if best_score >= threshold and best_col is not None:
        return best_col
    return None


def map_columns(df: pd.DataFrame, column_map: dict, config: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Mappa le colonne reali del DataFrame ai nostri nomi standardizzati.
    column_map: dict con {nome_standardizzato: [alias1, alias2, ...]}
    Restituisce dict {nome_standardizzato: colonna_reale_trovata} oppure None.
    """
    result = {}
    missing = []
    for std_name, aliases in column_map.items():
        found = find_column(df, aliases)
        if found:
            result[std_name] = found
        else:
            missing.append(std_name)
    if missing:
        # Prova fallback per colonne non trovate
        print(f"  ⚠️  Colonne non trovate: {', '.join(missing)}")
    return result


# ─────────────────────────── FILE DISCOVERY ──────────────────

def find_report_files(source_dir: Path, config: dict) -> tuple[str | None, str | None]:
    """Find WTS and VWT files using glob patterns from config."""
    patterns = config["file_patterns"]
    wts_file = None
    vwt_file = None

    for path in source_dir.glob("*.xlsx"):
        name_lower = path.name.lower()
        if wts_file is None:
            for pat in patterns["wts"]:
                # Convert glob-like "*vwts legacy*" to simple check
                pat_base = pat.strip("*").lower()
                if pat_base in name_lower:
                    wts_file = str(path)
                    break
        if vwt_file is None:
            for pat in patterns["vwt"]:
                pat_base = pat.strip("*").lower()
                if pat_base in name_lower:
                    vwt_file = str(path)
                    break
        if wts_file and vwt_file:
            break
    return wts_file, vwt_file


# ─────────────────────────── OUTPUT FILENAME ─────────────────

def generate_output_filename(data_dir: Path, config: dict) -> Path:
    """Generate output filename with date and incremental counter."""
    date_str = datetime.datetime.now().strftime("%y%m%d")
    prefix = config["output_filename"]["prefix"]
    ext = config["output_filename"]["extension"]
    pattern = re.compile(rf"{re.escape(prefix)}{re.escape(date_str)}-(\d+){re.escape(ext)}")
    existing = [f.name for f in data_dir.iterdir() if pattern.match(f.name)]
    if existing:
        nums = [int(pattern.match(name).group(1)) for name in existing]
        next_num = max(nums) + 1
    else:
        next_num = 1
    return data_dir / f"{prefix}{date_str}-{next_num}{ext}"


# ─────────────────────────── BU NORMALIZATION ────────────────

def _compile_bu_rules(config: dict) -> list:
    """Compile BU normalization regex rules from config."""
    rules = []
    for rule in config["bu_normalization"]["rules"]:
        try:
            rx = re.compile(rule["pattern"], re.IGNORECASE)
            rules.append((rx, rule["replacement"]))
        except re.error:
            print(f"  ⚠️  Regex error in BU rule: {rule['pattern']}")
    return rules


def normalize_bu(value: str, rules: list, default: str) -> str:
    """Normalize a BU value using compiled rules."""
    val = str(value).strip()
    if not val or val == "-":
        return default
    val_clean = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", val.upper()).replace("PROEJCT", "PROJECT")
    for rx, replacement in rules:
        if rx.search(val_clean):
            return replacement
    return default


# ─────────────────────────── SEGMENTATION ────────────────────

def get_segment(row: pd.Series, config: dict) -> str:
    """Determine market segment for a row."""
    name_up = str(row.get("FINAL Ship to Name", "")).upper()
    sic_code = str(row.get("FINAL SIC CODE", "")).strip()
    industry = str(row.get("Industry", "")).upper()
    industry_group = str(row.get("Industry Group", "")).strip().upper()
    source_file = str(row.get("Source file", ""))
    source_sic_text = str(row.get("Source_SIC_Text", "")).upper()
    overrides = config["special_client_overrides"]
    microe_kw = config.get("microe_keywords", {})

    # Lenovo is always Data Center
    if "LENOVO" in name_up:
        return "Data Center"

    # Check special client overrides
    special = _find_special_client(row, overrides)
    if special:
        return special

    # SIC starts with 737 → Data Center
    if sic_code.startswith("737"):
        return "Data Center"

    # VWT + Electronics Industry → Microelectronics
    if source_file == "VWT" and industry == "ELECTRONICS":
        return "Microelectronics"

    # Industry Group MICROE
    if industry_group == "MICROE":
        return "Microelectronics"

    # Keywords in Industry / SIC text
    ind_kw = microe_kw.get("industry", ["MICRO", "SEMICON"])
    sic_kw = microe_kw.get("sic_text", ["MICRO", "SEMICON"])
    for kw in ind_kw:
        if kw in industry:
            return "Microelectronics"
    for kw in sic_kw:
        if kw in source_sic_text:
            return "Microelectronics"

    # Data Center keywords in Industry
    if "DATA CENTER" in industry or "DATACENTER" in industry:
        return "Data Center"

    return "Other"


def _find_special_client(row: pd.Series, overrides: list) -> str | None:
    """Check if row matches a special client override."""
    name_up = str(row.get("FINAL Ship to Name", "")).upper()
    ship_to = str(row.get("FINAL SHIP TO ID", "")).strip()
    for client in overrides:
        ship_to_ids = client.get("ship_to_ids")
        name_contains = client.get("name_contains", "")
        if ship_to_ids is not None and ship_to in ship_to_ids:
            return client["segment"]
        if name_contains and name_contains.upper() in name_up:
            if ship_to_ids is None:
                return client["segment"]
            # With ship_to_ids: match only if ship_to matches too
            if ship_to in ship_to_ids:
                return client["segment"]
    return None


def is_epc(row: pd.Series, config: dict) -> bool:
    """Determine if a row is EPC."""
    name_up = str(row.get("FINAL Ship to Name", "")).upper()
    industry_group = str(row.get("Industry Group", "")).strip().upper()
    excluded_groups = set(g.upper() for g in config["excluded_industry_groups"])
    overrides = config["special_client_overrides"]

    if "LENOVO" in name_up:
        return False
    if industry_group in excluded_groups:
        return False
    return _find_special_client(row, overrides) is not None


# ─────────────────────────── EXCEL WRITING ───────────────────

def write_df_with_format(writer, df_to_write, sheet_name, table_name=None):
    """Write a DataFrame to Excel with formatting."""
    df_to_write = df_to_write.copy()
    df_to_write.columns = [str(c) for c in df_to_write.columns]
    df_to_write.to_excel(writer, sheet_name=sheet_name, index=False)
    sheet = writer.sheets[sheet_name]
    numeric_cols = [
        c for c in df_to_write.select_dtypes(include=["number"]).columns
        if not pd.api.types.is_bool_dtype(df_to_write[c])
    ]
    for col_idx, col_name in enumerate(df_to_write.columns, start=1):
        if col_name in numeric_cols:
            for row_idx in range(2, len(df_to_write) + 2):
                sheet.cell(row=row_idx, column=col_idx).number_format = "#,##0"
    if table_name:
        last_col = get_column_letter(len(df_to_write.columns))
        last_row = len(df_to_write) + 1
        table_ref = f"A1:{last_col}{last_row}"
        table = Table(displayName=table_name, ref=table_ref)
        style = TableStyleInfo(
            name="TableStyleMedium9",
            showFirstColumn=False,
            showLastColumn=False,
            showRowStripes=True,
            showColumnStripes=False,
        )
        table.tableStyleInfo = style
        sheet.add_table(table)


def build_pivot_with_totals(source_df, row_key, col_key):
    """Build a pivot table with totals."""
    pivot = source_df.groupby([row_key, col_key], dropna=False)["EUR"].sum().unstack(fill_value=0)
    pivot["_Total"] = pivot.sum(axis=1)
    total_row = pivot.sum(numeric_only=True).to_frame().T
    total_row.index = ["Totale"]
    pivot = pd.concat([pivot, total_row])
    pivot = pivot.reset_index()
    return pivot


def build_top10_per_bu(source_df, row_key="FINAL Ship to Name", top_n=10, bu_order=None):
    """Build top-10 customers per BU."""
    if bu_order is None:
        bu_order = ["CSM", "EPP", "HQ", "MWIS", "T&P"]
    agg = (
        source_df
        .groupby([row_key, "BU"], dropna=False)["EUR"]
        .sum()
        .reset_index()
    )
    agg["rank"] = agg.groupby("BU")["EUR"].rank(method="first", ascending=False)
    top = agg[agg["rank"] <= top_n].copy()
    pivot = top.pivot(index=row_key, columns="BU", values="EUR").fillna(0)
    for bu in bu_order:
        if bu not in pivot.columns:
            pivot[bu] = 0.0
    pivot = pivot[bu_order]
    pivot["Total"] = pivot.sum(axis=1)
    total_row = {row_key: "Totale"}
    for bu in bu_order:
        total_row[bu] = pivot[bu].sum()
    total_row["Total"] = pivot["Total"].sum()
    pivot = pivot.sort_values("Total", ascending=False)
    pivot = pivot.reset_index()
    total_df = pd.DataFrame([total_row])
    pivot = pd.concat([pivot, total_df], ignore_index=True)
    return pivot


def build_top10_per_year(source_df, row_key="FINAL Ship to Name", top_n=10):
    """Build top-10 customers per year."""
    agg = (
        source_df
        .groupby([row_key, "Year"], dropna=False)["EUR"]
        .sum()
        .reset_index()
    )
    total = agg.groupby(row_key)["EUR"].sum().reset_index()
    top_customers = total.nlargest(top_n, "EUR")[row_key]
    top = agg[agg[row_key].isin(top_customers)].copy()
    pivot = top.pivot(index=row_key, columns="Year", values="EUR").fillna(0)
    years = sorted(pivot.columns.tolist())
    pivot = pivot[years]
    pivot["Total"] = pivot.sum(axis=1)
    total_row = {row_key: "Totale"}
    for year in years:
        total_row[year] = pivot[year].sum()
    total_row["Total"] = pivot["Total"].sum()
    pivot = pivot.sort_values("Total", ascending=False)
    pivot = pivot.reset_index()
    total_df = pd.DataFrame([total_row])
    pivot = pd.concat([pivot, total_df], ignore_index=True)
    return pivot


# ─────────────────────────── MAIN ────────────────────────────

def main():
    cfg = load_config()

    print("🔧 Filter MicroE/DC — config-driven engine")
    print(f"   Config: {CONFIG_PATH}")

    # ── 1. DETERMINE INPUT FILES ──
    if len(sys.argv) >= 3:
        FILE_1 = sys.argv[1]
        FILE_2 = sys.argv[2]
        print(f"📥 File da argomenti:\n   WTS: {FILE_1}\n   VWT: {FILE_2}")
    else:
        # Try Gmail fetcher
        try:
            from fetch_gmail_reports import SalesReportFetcher

            fetcher = SalesReportFetcher(download_dir=str(SOURCE_DIR))
            FILE_1, FILE_2 = fetcher.fetch_latest_reports()
            print(f"📥 File da Gmail:\n   WTS: {FILE_1}\n   VWT: {FILE_2}")
        except Exception:
            file_1, file_2 = find_report_files(SOURCE_DIR, cfg)
            if file_1 and file_2:
                print(f"⚠️  Gmail fallback: uso file dalla directory source/")
                FILE_1, FILE_2 = file_1, file_2
                print(f"   WTS: {FILE_1}\n   VWT: {FILE_2}")
            else:
                print("❌ Errore: file non trovati né via Gmail né in source/.")
                sys.exit(1)

    if not os.path.exists(FILE_1) or not os.path.exists(FILE_2):
        print(f"❌ Errore: file non trovati:\n   {FILE_1}\n   {FILE_2}")
        sys.exit(1)

    # ── 2. OUTPUT FILENAME ──
    OUTPUT_FILE = generate_output_filename(DATA_DIR, cfg)
    print(f"📄 Report output: {OUTPUT_FILE}")

    # ── 3. ELABORAZIONE WTS ──
    print(f"\n📋 Elaborazione WTS ({Path(FILE_1).name})...")
    df_wts = pd.read_excel(FILE_1)
    wts_map = map_columns(df_wts, cfg["wts_columns"], cfg)
    if not wts_map:
        print("❌ Errore: impossibile mappare colonne WTS. Verifica schema_config.yaml")
        sys.exit(1)

    col = cfg["output_columns"]
    wts_standard = pd.DataFrame()
    wts_standard[col["bu"]] = df_wts[wts_map["hierarchy_1"]]
    wts_standard[col["customer_name"]] = df_wts[wts_map["customer_name"]]
    wts_standard[col["ship_to_id"]] = df_wts[wts_map["ship_to_id"]]
    wts_standard[col["city_ship_to"]] = df_wts[wts_map["city_ship_to"]]
    wts_standard[col["sic_code"]] = df_wts[wts_map["sic_code"]]
    wts_standard[col["ship_to_country"]] = df_wts[wts_map["ship_to_country"]]
    wts_standard[col["sub_industry"]] = df_wts[wts_map["sub_industry"]]
    wts_standard[col["industry"]] = df_wts[wts_map["industry"]]
    wts_standard[col["industry_group"]] = df_wts[wts_map["industry_group"]]
    wts_standard[col["year"]] = df_wts[wts_map["year"]]
    wts_standard[col["month"]] = df_wts[wts_map["month"]]
    wts_standard[col["revenue_eur"]] = pd.to_numeric(
        df_wts[wts_map["revenue_eur"]], errors="coerce"
    ).fillna(0.0)
    wts_standard[col["source_file"]] = "WTS"
    # BU from hierarchy_1
    wts_standard["BU_src"] = df_wts[wts_map["hierarchy_1"]].fillna("")

    # ── 4. ELABORAZIONE VWT ──
    print(f"📋 Elaborazione VWT ({Path(FILE_2).name})...")
    xl = pd.ExcelFile(FILE_2)
    vwt_sheet = cfg["sheet_names"]["vwt"]
    df_vwt_raw = pd.read_excel(xl, sheet_name=vwt_sheet)
    df_vwt_raw.columns = [str(c).strip() for c in df_vwt_raw.columns]

    vwt_map = map_columns(df_vwt_raw, cfg["vwt_columns"], cfg)
    if not vwt_map:
        print("❌ Errore: impossibile mappare colonne VWT. Verifica schema_config.yaml")
        sys.exit(1)

    vwt_standard = pd.DataFrame()
    vwt_standard[col["bu"]] = ""
    vwt_standard[col["customer_name"]] = df_vwt_raw[vwt_map["customer_name"]].fillna("")
    vwt_standard[col["ship_to_id"]] = ""
    vwt_standard[col["city_ship_to"]] = ""
    vwt_standard[col["sic_code"]] = df_vwt_raw[vwt_map["customer_sic"]].fillna("")
    vwt_standard[col["ship_to_country"]] = df_vwt_raw[vwt_map["country_name"]].fillna("")
    vwt_standard[col["sub_industry"]] = ""
    vwt_standard[col["industry"]] = df_vwt_raw[vwt_map["customer_market"]].fillna("")
    vwt_standard[col["industry_group"]] = ""

    # SIC Code detail (if available)
    if "customer_sic_code" in vwt_map:
        vwt_standard[col["sic_code"]] = df_vwt_raw[vwt_map["customer_sic_code"]].astype(str).fillna("")
    vwt_standard["Source_SIC_Text"] = df_vwt_raw[vwt_map["source_sic_text"]].fillna("")

    # Default year/month for VWT
    vwt_defaults = cfg.get("vwt_defaults", {"year": 2026, "month": 1})
    vwt_standard[col["year"]] = vwt_defaults["year"]
    vwt_standard[col["month"]] = vwt_defaults["month"]
    vwt_standard[col["revenue_eur"]] = pd.to_numeric(
        df_vwt_raw[vwt_map["revenue_eur"]], errors="coerce"
    ).fillna(0.0)
    vwt_standard[col["source_file"]] = "VWT"

    # BU column for VWT — try multiple aliases
    bu_col = find_column(df_vwt_raw, cfg["vwt_columns"]["bu_column"])
    if bu_col:
        vwt_standard["BU_src"] = df_vwt_raw[bu_col].fillna("")
    else:
        # Fallback: positional index (last resort)
        print(f"  ⚠️  Colonna BU non trovata per VWT, uso fallback posizionale")
        vwt_standard["BU_src"] = df_vwt_raw.iloc[:, 13].astype(str).fillna("")

    # ── 5. UNIONE ──
    df = pd.concat([wts_standard, vwt_standard], ignore_index=True)
    df[col["revenue_eur"]] = pd.to_numeric(
        df[col["revenue_eur"]].astype(str).str.replace(",", ".", regex=False),
        errors="coerce",
    ).fillna(0.0)

    # Pulizia stringhe
    df[col["industry_group"]] = df[col["industry_group"]].astype(str).str.strip().str.upper()
    df[col["industry"]] = df[col["industry"]].astype(str).str.strip()
    df[col["sic_code"]] = df[col["sic_code"]].astype(str).str.strip()
    df[col["customer_name"]] = df[col["customer_name"]].fillna("").astype(str)
    df["BU_src"] = df["BU_src"].astype(str).str.strip()

    # Normalizza BU
    bu_rules = _compile_bu_rules(cfg)
    bu_default = cfg["bu_normalization"]["default"]
    df[col["bu"]] = df["BU_src"].apply(lambda v: normalize_bu(v, bu_rules, bu_default))
    df.drop(columns=["BU_src"], inplace=True)

    # ── 6. SEGMENTAZIONE ──
    df[col["market_segment"]] = df.apply(lambda r: get_segment(r, cfg), axis=1)

    # Filtro: solo Microelectronics e Data Center
    df_filtered = df[
        df[col["market_segment"]].isin(["Microelectronics", "Data Center"])
    ].copy()

    # EPC identification
    df_filtered[col["epc"]] = df_filtered.apply(lambda r: is_epc(r, cfg), axis=1)

    # ── 7. FINAL FORMAT ──
    output_order = cfg["output_column_order"]
    # Ensure all columns exist
    for c in output_order:
        if c not in df_filtered.columns:
            df_filtered[c] = ""
    df_export = df_filtered[output_order].copy()

    # Sorting
    df_export = df_export.sort_values(
        [col["market_segment"], col["year"], col["month"], col["customer_name"]]
    )

    # ── 8. EXPORT ──
    print(f"\n📤 Scrittura report: {OUTPUT_FILE}")
    with pd.ExcelWriter(OUTPUT_FILE, engine="openpyxl") as writer:
        write_df_with_format(writer, df_export, "Report", table_name="ReportTable")

        df_micro = df_filtered[df_filtered[col["market_segment"]] == "Microelectronics"]
        df_dc = df_filtered[df_filtered[col["market_segment"]] == "Data Center"]

        bu_order = cfg.get("bu_order", ["CSM", "EPP", "HQ", "MWIS", "T&P"])

        customers_micro = build_pivot_with_totals(df_micro, col["customer_name"], col["year"])
        customers_dc = build_pivot_with_totals(df_dc, col["customer_name"], col["year"])
        write_df_with_format(writer, customers_micro, "Customers_MicroE_Year", table_name="Customers_MicroE_Year_Table")
        write_df_with_format(writer, customers_dc, "Customers_DC_Year", table_name="Customers_DC_Year_Table")

        countries_micro = build_pivot_with_totals(df_micro, col["ship_to_country"], col["bu"])
        countries_dc = build_pivot_with_totals(df_dc, col["ship_to_country"], col["bu"])
        write_df_with_format(writer, countries_micro, "Country_MicroE_BU", table_name="Country_MicroE_BU_Table")
        write_df_with_format(writer, countries_dc, "Country_DC_BU", table_name="Country_DC_BU_Table")

        top_by_bu_micro = build_top10_per_bu(df_micro, col["customer_name"], top_n=10, bu_order=bu_order)
        top_by_bu_dc = build_top10_per_bu(df_dc, col["customer_name"], top_n=10, bu_order=bu_order)
        write_df_with_format(writer, top_by_bu_micro, "Top10_by_BU_MicroE", table_name="Top10_BU_MicroE_Table")
        write_df_with_format(writer, top_by_bu_dc, "Top10_by_BU_DC", table_name="Top10_BU_DC_Table")

        top_by_year_micro = build_top10_per_year(df_micro, col["customer_name"], top_n=10)
        top_by_year_dc = build_top10_per_year(df_dc, col["customer_name"], top_n=10)
        write_df_with_format(writer, top_by_year_micro, "Top10_by_Year_MicroE", table_name="Top10_Year_MicroE_Table")
        write_df_with_format(writer, top_by_year_dc, "Top10_by_Year_DC", table_name="Top10_Year_DC_Table")

    print(f"\n✅ Report completato: {OUTPUT_FILE}")
    print(f"📊 Record totali: {len(df_export)}")
    micro_count = len(df_export[df_export[col["market_segment"]] == "Microelectronics"])
    dc_count = len(df_export[df_export[col["market_segment"]] == "Data Center"])
    epc_count = df_export[col["epc"]].sum()
    print(f"   ├─ Microelectronics: {micro_count}")
    print(f"   ├─ Data Center: {dc_count}")
    print(f"   └─ Clienti EPC: {epc_count}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
