{"content": "     1|---
     2|name: 5-revenuesRep
     3|description: >
     4| Extracts and filters sales data for Microelectronics (Industry Group: "MICROE") and Data Center (SIC CODE: "737...") markets from Excel reports.
     5| It identifies EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) clients using keyword matching and consolidates multi-source data into a single filtered Excel report.
     6| Generates a structured Excel file with market segmentation, EPC flagging, and auto-sends the result to alfredo.di-blasi@veolia.com.
     7| Accessible via command "5" or "revenues-rep".
     8|tags:
     9| - sales
    10| - reporting
    11| - automation
    12| - microe
    13| - datacenter
    14| - gmail
    15|version: 0.2.0
    16|visibility: private
    17|user-invocable: true
    18|slash-commands:
    19| - /5
    20| - /revenues-rep
    21|command-dispatch: tool
    22|command-tool: shell
    23|command-arg-mode: raw
    24|---
    25|
    26|# MicroE & Data Center Sales Extraction (v0.2.0)
    27|
    28|This skill extracts, filters, and consolidates sales data from multiple Excel reports, focusing on Microelectronics and Data Center markets.
    29|
    30|## When to Use
    31|
    32|✅ **USE this skill when:**
    33|- You need to extract sales data for Microelectronics (Industry Group = "MICROE")
    34|- You need to extract sales data for Data Centers (SIC CODE starting with "737")
    35|- You want to identify EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) clients
    36|- New files arrive from apsminerva@gmail.com (Vendite email)
    37|- You need to consolidate data from multiple sales reports into one filtered file
    38|- You want the report automatically emailed to alfredo.di-blasi@veolia.com
    39|
    40|❌ **DON'T use this skill when:**
    41|- You need real-time data from ERP systems
    42|- You're working with non-Excel data sources
    43|
    44|## Pipeline
    45|
    46|```
    47|Nuovi file da apsminerva (Gmail)
    48|        │
    49|        ▼
    50|  1. Download automatico via fetch_gmail_reports.py
    51|        │
    52|        ▼
    53|  2. (Se nuovo formato colonne) Adattamento via adapt_new_files.py
    54|        │
    55|        ▼
    56|  3. Filtro MicroE + Data Center via filter_microe_dc.py
    57|        │
    58|        ▼
    59|  4. Output: ME-DC-salesReport-YYMMDD-N.xlsx
    60|        │
    61|        ▼
    62|  5. Invio email automatico a alfredo.di-blasi@veolia.com
    63|```
    64|
    65|## Input Files
    66|
    67|The skill fetches from Gmail (apsminerva@gmail.com), looking for:
    68|
    69|| Tipo | Pattern nome file | Contenuto |
    70||------|------------------|-----------|
    71|| **VWTS** | `VWTS legacy Sales *.xlsx` | Dati transazioni vendite |
    72|| **VWT** | `*Customer Spend*` / `Copy of Customer Spend*.xlsx` | Dati spesa clienti |
    73|
    74|### Formato colonne supportati
    75|
    76|**Formato classico** (usato fino a Marzo 2026):
    77|- `hierarchy_1`, `FINAL Ship to Name`, `FINAL SHIP TO ID`, `FINAL CITY SHIP TO`
    78|- `FINAL SIC CODE`, `FINAL SHIP TO COUNTRY`, `SubIndustry`, `Industry`
    79|- `Industry Group`, `Year of Posting Date in the Document`, `Month of Posting Date in the Document`
    80|- `EUR Veolia P&L`
    81|
    82|**Nuovo formato** (da Aprile 2026 in poi):
    83|
    84|VWTS Sales:
    85|- `Managerial BU`, `Customer Name`, `Customer Number`, `DUNS Number`, `Postal Code`
    86|- `City`, `Customer SIC Code`, `Country Name`, `Customer SIC`, `Customer Market Code`
    87|- `Customer Market`, `Vector Split`, `Revenus EUR`, `Year`, `Month`
    88|
    89|VWT Customer Spend:
    90|- `Country Name`, `Company Code`, `Customer Name`, `Customer Number`, `DUNS Number`
    91|- `Postal Code`, `City`, `Customer SIC Code`, `Country Name.1`, `Customer SIC`
    92|- `Customer Market Code`, `Customer Market`, `Vector Split`, `Vector Split (Adapted)`
    93|- `WT Business Line (CC11)`, `WT Business Line code (CC11)`, `Revenus EUR`
    94|
    95|## Filtering Criteria
    96|
    97|### Microelectronics
    98|- **Filter:** `Industry Group` = "MICROE" (new format: `Customer Market` = "MicroE" or `Customer Market Code` = "Electronics")
    99|- **SIC override:** 737... prefix for Data Centers
   100|- **Special client overrides:** Exyte, Arcade Engineering, STMicro, Analog Devices, Membrane, Chemco (Ireland), etc.
   101|
   102|### Data Center
   103|- **Filter:** `SIC CODE` starts with "737" (e.g., 7374 - Computer Processing and Data Preparation Services)
   104|- **Special client overrides:** Dornan, Jacobs Clean Energy, Crystal Computing, Green Box Computing, etc.
   105|
   106|### EPC Client Identification
   107|
   108|Flags potential EPC/Construction clients using keyword matching:
   109|
   110|**EPC Keywords:** PROJECT, EPC, CONSTRUCTION, ENGINEERING, CHEZ, BILFINGER, EXYTE, EDAR, VINCI, BESIX, BOUYGUES, SAIPEM, TECNIMONT, MOTA-ENGIL, GROHE, FLUOR, JACOBS, BLACK & VEATCH
   111|
   112|**Detection:** Client name contains any EPC keyword (case-insensitive)
   113|
   114|## Output
   115|
   116|### Generated File: `data/ME-DC-salesReport-YYMMDD-N.xlsx`
   117|
   118|**15 colonne nell'ordine:**
   119|| Col | Nome | Descrizione |
   120||-----|------|-------------|
   121|| A | BU | Business Unit (CSM/EPP/HQ/MWIS/T&P) |
   122|| B | FINAL Ship to Name | Nome cliente finale |
   123|| C | FINAL SHIP TO ID | ID cliente |
   124|| D | FINAL CITY SHIP TO | Città |
   125|| E | FINAL SIC CODE | Codice SIC |
   126|| F | FINAL SHIP TO COUNTRY | Paese |
   127|| G | SubIndustry | Sotto-industria |
   128|| H | Industry | Industria |
   129|| I | Industry Group | Gruppo industriale |
   130|| J | Year | Anno |
   131|| K | Month | Mese |
   132|| L | EUR | Ricavi in EUR |
   133|| M | Source file | WTS o VWT |
   134|| N | Market_segment | Microelectronics o Data Center |
   135|| O | EPC | True/False |
   136|
   137|**Sorting:** Market Segment → Year → Month → Client Name
   138|
   139|## Email Delivery
   140|
   141|All'invio dello script principale, il report filtrato viene automaticamente spedito via Gmail a **alfredo.di-blasi@veolia.com**.
   142|
   143|Lo script `send_report_email.py` può anche essere usato standalone:
   144|
   145|```bash
   146|python send_report_email.py data/ME-DC-salesReport-260510-1.xlsx
   147|```
   148|
   149|## Files dello Skill
   150|
   151|| File | Ruolo |
   152||------|-------|
   153|| `filter_microe_dc.py` | Script principale: filtro, segmentazione, EPC |
   154|| `fetch_gmail_reports.py` | Download da Gmail (formato classico) |
   155|| `adapt_new_files.py` | Adattatore per nuovi formati colonne → li passa al filter |
   156|| `send_report_email.py` | Invio email con allegato |
   157|| `data/` | Cartella output report filtrati |
   158|| `source/` | Cartella file sorgente (copia locale) |
   159|
   160|## Usage Examples
   161|
   162|### Example 1: Full pipeline (fetch + filter + email)
   163|
   164|```bash
   165|# 1. Scarica i file da apsminerva@gmail.com
   166|python fetch_gmail_reports.py data
   167|
   168|# 2. Filtra (se formato classico)
   169|python filter_microe_dc.py "data/VWTS legacy Sales *.xlsx" "data/Copy of Customer Spend*.xlsx"
   170|
   171|# 3. Oppure se nuovo formato
   172|python adapt_new_files.py "data/VWTS legacy Sales Jan2013-April2026.xlsx" "data/Copy of Customer Spend EUROPE 04-26.xlsx"
   173|
   174|# 4. Invia email
   175|python send_report_email.py "data/ME-DC-salesReport-260510-1.xlsx"
   176|```
   177|
   178|### Example 2: Alfredo dice "applica la skill 5"
   179|
   180|Astro automaticamente:
   181|1. Trova i nuovi files in `data/` o li scarica da Gmail
   182|2. Rileva il formato colonne (classico o nuovo)
   183|3. Applica adapt_new_files.py se necessario
   184|4. Esegue filter_microe_dc.py
   185|5. Invia il report via email
   186|
   187|## Troubleshooting
   188|
   189|### "Could not find both required files"
   190|→ I nuovi files hanno nomi diversi. Usa lo script manuale per scaricare:
   191|  Cerca l'email "Vendite" da apsminerva e scarica i 2 allegati.
   192|
   193|### "Worksheet named 'Detail per customer ( Ship to )' not found"
   194|→ Il file VWT ha un formato diverso. Usa `adapt_new_files.py` che gestisce sia sheet unico che multi-sheet.
   195|
   196|### Colonna `hierarchy_1` non trovata / `FINAL Ship to Name` non trovata
   197|→ Il file VWTS ha il nuovo formato (Aprile 2026+). Usa `adapt_new_files.py`.
   198|
   199|### Errori di memoria / `KeyError`
   200|→ Verifica che i file non siano corrotti. I file da 10+ MB richiedono RAM sufficiente.

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"content": "---
name: 5-revenuesRep
description: >
 Extracts and filters sales data for Microelectronics and Data Center markets
 from multi-source Excel reports using a config-driven engine. All column
 mappings, file patterns, segment overrides, and BU rules live in
 schema_config.yaml. Python code is stable — edit YAML when finance changes format.
tags:
 - sales
 - reporting
 - automation
 - microe
 - datacenter
 - gmail
version: 0.3.0
visibility: private
user-invocable: true
slash-commands:
 - /5
 - /revenues-rep
command-dispatch: tool
command-tool: shell
command-arg-mode: raw
---

# MicroE & Data Center Sales Extraction (v0.3.0) — Config-Driven

## What Changed in v0.3.0

The skill was refactored to separate **configuration** from **logic**:

- **`schema_config.yaml`** → editable by OpenClaw. Contains all column mappings,
  file patterns, sheet names, segment overrides, BU rules, EPC keywords.
- **`filter_microe_dc.py`** → stable Python engine. Reads `schema_config.yaml`
  at runtime. Modified only by Hermes for new business logic.

When the finance team changes report format (column renames, new sheets, etc.),
**only `schema_config.yaml` needs editing** — no Python changes required.

## How the Config Engine Works

### Fuzzy Column Matching

Instead of exact column names, the engine uses **token-overlap matching** (Jaccard
similarity). A column in the Excel file is matched if its token overlap with an
alias in `schema_config.yaml` is ≥ 0.6 (60%). This means:

- `FINAL Ship to Name` matches `Final Ship to Name`, `Ship-to Name`, `Ship to Name`
- `EUR Veolia P&L` matches `Revenue EUR`, `EUR Revenue`, `EUR`
- `Year of Posting Date in the Document` matches `Year`, `Fiscal Year`

### Priority: First matching alias wins, exact match wins immediately.

---

## When to Use

✅ **USE this skill when:**
- You need to extract sales data for Microelectronics and Data Center markets
- New files arrive from apsminerva@gmail.com (Vendite email)
- You need to consolidate data from two reports into one filtered file
- You want the report emailed to alfredo.di-blasi@veolia.com

❌ **DON'T use this skill when:**
- You need real-time data from ERP systems
- You're working with non-Excel data sources

---

## Pipeline

```
Nuovi file da apsminerva (Gmail)
        │
        ▼
  1. Download via fetch_gmail_reports.py
        │
        ▼
  2. filter_microe_dc.py legge schema_config.yaml
     │  - mappa colonne con fuzzy matching
     │  - unisce WTS + VWT in formato standard
        │
        ▼
  3. Segmentazione (MicroE / Data Center / EPC)
        │
        ▼
  4. Output: ME-DC-salesReport-YYMMDD-N.xlsx
        │
        ▼
  5. Invio email a alfredo.di-blasi@veolia.com
```

---

## Input Files

The skill fetches from Gmail (apsminerva@gmail.com), looking for:

| Tipo | Pattern (da schema_config.yaml) | Contenuto |
|------|-------------------------------|-----------|
| **WTS** | `*vwts legacy*.xlsx` / `*WTS*.xlsx` | Dati transazioni vendite (SFDC1) |
| **VWT** | `*vwt legacy*.xlsx` / `*VWT*.xlsx` | Dati spesa clienti (SFDC2) |

> Se i pattern cambiano, modifica `file_patterns` in `schema_config.yaml`.

---

## Configuration Reference (schema_config.yaml)

### 1. `wts_columns` / `vwt_columns`

Mappa le colonne dei file Excel ai nostri nomi standardizzati. Ogni entry ha
una lista di alias (case-insensitive, fuzzy match):

```yaml
wts_columns:
  customer_name:
    - "FINAL Ship to Name"     # primo alias = nome atteso
    - "Final Ship to Name"
    - "Ship to Name"
    - "Customer Name"          # ultima risorsa
  revenue_eur:
    - "EUR Veolia P&L"
    - "Revenue EUR"
    - "EUR"
```

**Quando il finance cambia nome a una colonna:** AGGIUNGI il nuovo nome come
alias nella lista. Il fuzzy matching lo troverà.

### 2. `file_patterns`

Glob patterns per riconoscere automaticamente i due file nella directory source/:

```yaml
file_patterns:
  wts:
    - "*vwts legacy*.xlsx"
    - "*WTS*.xlsx"
  vwt:
    - "*vwt legacy*.xlsx"
    - "*VWT*.xlsx"
```

### 3. `sheet_names`

Nome del foglio Excel per VWT (il file WTS usa il foglio attivo):

```yaml
sheet_names:
  vwt: "Detail per customer ( Ship to )"
```

### 4. `special_client_overrides`

Clienti con segmento forzato (override della logica automatica):

```yaml
special_client_overrides:
  - name_contains: "EXYTE"
    segment: "Microelectronics"
  - name_contains: "CRYSTAL COMPUTING"
    segment: "Data Center"
    ship_to_ids: ["4000245814", "4000194913"]
```

- **Con `ship_to_ids`**: matcha solo se Ship-to ID corrisponde
- **Senza `ship_to_ids`**: matcha solo per nome

### 5. `excluded_industry_groups`

Industry Group esclusi dalla classificazione EPC:

```yaml
excluded_industry_groups:
  - "CPI"
  - "PHARMA"
  - "POWER"
```

### 6. `microe_keywords`

Keywords per rilevare Microelectronics in Industry / SIC text:

```yaml
microe_keywords:
  industry:
    - "MICRO"
    - "SEMICON"
  sic_text:
    - "MICRO"
    - "SEMICON"
```

### 7. `bu_normalization`

Regole di normalizzazione BU (regex → replacement):

```yaml
bu_normalization:
  default: "HQ"
  rules:
    - pattern: "EPP|ES PROJECT"
      replacement: "EPP"
```

### 8. `output_columns` / `output_column_order`

Nomi e ordine delle colonne nel report finale. Modifica solo se vuoi
rinominare o riordinare le colonne di output.

### 9. `vwt_defaults`

Anno/mese di default per VWT (quando non leggibili dalle colonne):

```yaml
vwt_defaults:
  year: 2026
  month: 1
```

---

## Files dello Skill

| File | Ruolo | Editabile da |
|------|-------|-------------|
| `schema_config.yaml` | Configurazione: colonne, pattern, override, regole | **OpenClaw** ✅ |
| `filter_microe_dc.py` | Engine Python stabile — usa YAML per tutto | **Hermes** 🔧 |
| `fetch_gmail_reports.py` | Download da Gmail | OpenClaw |
| `send_report_email.py` | Invio email con allegato | OpenClaw |
| `data/` | Cartella output report | — |
| `source/` | Cartella file sorgente | — |

---

## For OpenClaw: How to Edit schema_config.yaml

### Scenario 1: Finance changed a column name

Find the relevant section in `schema_config.yaml` and **add the new name** as
an alias. The fuzzy matching handles minor variations.

**Esempio:** Se "EUR Veolia P&L" diventa "Revenue in EUR":

```yaml
wts_columns:
  revenue_eur:
    - "EUR Veolia P&L"        # vecchio nome (lascia)
    - "Revenue EUR"           # già presente
    - "Revenue in EUR"        # ← NUOVO (aggiungi qui)
```

### Scenario 2: Finance added/renamed a sheet

```yaml
sheet_names:
  vwt: "Nuovo Nome Foglio"    # ← cambia qui
```

### Scenario 3: New special client override

```yaml
special_client_overrides:
  - name_contains: "NUOVO CLIENTE"
    segment: "Microelectronics"
```

### Scenario 4: File naming pattern changed

```yaml
file_patterns:
  wts:
    - "*nuovo-pattern*.xlsx"   # ← aggiungi prima degli altri
```

> **Regola d'oro:** Non toccare `filter_microe_dc.py`. Se la modifica
> richiede cambiamenti al Python, chiama Hermes.

---

## Output

### Generated File: `data/ME-DC-salesReport-YYMMDD-N.xlsx`

**15 colonne nell'ordine:**

| Col | Nome | Descrizione |
|-----|------|-------------|
| A | BU | Business Unit (CSM/EPP/HQ/MWIS/T&P) |
| B | FINAL Ship to Name | Nome cliente finale |
| C | FINAL SHIP TO ID | ID cliente |
| D | FINAL CITY SHIP TO | Città |
| E | FINAL SIC CODE | Codice SIC |
| F | FINAL SHIP TO COUNTRY | Paese |
| G | SubIndustry | Sotto-industria |
| H | Industry | Industria |
| I | Industry Group | Gruppo industriale |
| J | Year | Anno |
| K | Month | Mese |
| L | EUR | Ricavi in EUR |
| M | Source file | WTS o VWT |
| N | Market_segment | Microelectronics o Data Center |
| O | EPC | True/False |

---

## Usage Examples

### Full pipeline

```bash
# 1. Scarica i file da apsminerva@gmail.com
python fetch_gmail_reports.py data

# 2. Filtra con motore config-driven
python filter_microe_dc.py "source/VWTS legacy Sales.xlsx" "source/Copy of Customer Spend.xlsx"

# 3. Invia email
python send_report_email.py "data/ME-DC-salesReport-260510-1.xlsx"
```

### Alfredo dice "applica la skill 5"

1. Trova/scarica i file in `source/`
2. `filter_microe_dc.py` carica `schema_config.yaml`
3. Il fuzzy matching mappa le colonne qualsiasi sia il nome esatto
4. Genera report filtrato
5. Invia via email

2. **File ridenominati** → aggiorna `file_patterns`

5. **Nuova regola BU** → aggiungi in `bu_normalization.rules`

Il Python non va più toccato per cambi di formato. Solo Hermes lo modifica per nuova logica di business.

messages.content:
Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

{"success": true, "name": "hermes-agent-skill-authoring", "description": "Author in-repo SKILL.md: frontmatter, validator, structure.", "tags": ["skills", "authoring", "hermes-agent", "conventions", "skill-md"], "related_skills": ["writing-plans", "requesting-code-review"], "content": "---

description: "Author in-repo SKILL.md: frontmatter, validator, structure."
version: 1.0.0
author: Hermes Agent

platforms: [linux, macos, windows]

    tags: [skills, authoring, hermes-agent, conventions, skill-md]
    related_skills: [writing-plans, requesting-code-review]

# Authoring Hermes-Agent Skills (in-repo)

## Overview

There are two places a SKILL.md can live:

1. **User-local:** `~/.hermes/skills/<maybe-category>/<name>/SKILL.md` — personal, not shared. Created via `skill_manage(action='create')`.
2. **In-repo (this skill is about this case):** `/home/bb/hermes-agent/skills/<category>/<name>/SKILL.md` — committed, shipped with the package. Use `write_file` + `git add`. `skill_manage(action='create')` does NOT target this tree.

## When to Use

- User asks you to add a skill "in this branch / repo / commit"
- You're committing a reusable workflow that should ship with hermes-agent
- You're editing an existing skill under `/home/bb/hermes-agent/skills/` (use `patch` for small edits, `write_file` for rewrites; `skill_manage` still works for patch on in-repo skills, but not for `create`)

## Required Frontmatter

Source of truth: `tools/skill_manager_tool.py::_validate_frontmatter`. Hard requirements:

- Starts with `---` as the first bytes (no leading blank line).
- Closes with `\n---\n` before the body.
- Parses as a YAML mapping.
- `name` field present.
- `description` field present, ≤ **1024 chars** (`MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH`).
- Non-empty body after the closing `---`.

Peer-matched shape used by every skill under `skills/software-development/`:

```yaml
---
name: my-skill-name               # lowercase, hyphens, ≤64 chars (MAX_NAME_LENGTH)
description: Use when <trigger>. <one-line behavior>.
version: 1.0.0
author: Hermes Agent
license: MIT
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [short, descriptive, tags]
    related_skills: [other-skill, another-skill]
---
```

`version` / `author` / `license` / `metadata` are NOT enforced by the validator, but every peer has them — omit and your skill sticks out.

## Size Limits

- Description: ≤ 1024 chars (enforced).
- Full SKILL.md: ≤ 100,000 chars (enforced as `MAX_SKILL_CONTENT_CHARS`, ~36k tokens).
- Peer skills in `software-development/` sit at **8-14k chars**. Aim for that range. If you're pushing past 20k, split into `references/*.md` and reference them from SKILL.md.

## Peer-Matched Structure

Every in-repo skill follows roughly:

```
# <Title>

## Overview
One or two paragraphs: what and why.

## When to Use
- Bulleted triggers
- "Don't use for:" counter-triggers

## <Topic sections specific to the skill>
- Quick-reference tables are common
- Code blocks with exact commands
- Hermes-specific recipes (tests via scripts/run_tests.sh, ui-tui paths, etc.)

## Common Pitfalls
Numbered list of mistakes and their fixes.

## Verification Checklist
- [ ] Checkbox list of post-action verifications

## One-Shot Recipes (optional)
Named scenarios → concrete command sequences.
```

Not every section is mandatory, but `Overview` + `When to Use` + actionable body + pitfalls are the minimum for the skill to feel like a peer.

## Directory Placement

```
skills/<category>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
```

Categories currently in repo (confirm with `ls skills/`): `autonomous-ai-agents`, `creative`, `data-science`, `devops`, `dogfood`, `email`, `gaming`, `github`, `leisure`, `mcp`, `media`, `mlops/*`, `note-taking`, `productivity`, `red-teaming`, `research`, `smart-home`, `social-media`, `software-development`.

Pick the closest existing category. Don't invent new top-level categories casually.

## Workflow

1. **Survey peers** in the target category:
   ```
   ls skills/<category>/
   ```
   Read 2-3 peer SKILL.md files to match tone and structure.
2. **Check validator constraints** in `tools/skill_manager_tool.py` if unsure.
3. **Draft** with `write_file` to `skills/<category>/<name>/SKILL.md`.
4. **Validate locally**:
   ```python
   import yaml, re, pathlib
   content = pathlib.Path("skills/<category>/<name>/SKILL.md").read_text()
   assert content.startswith("---")
   m = re.search(r'\n---\s*\n', content[3:])
   fm = yaml.safe_load(content[3:m.start()+3])
   assert "name" in fm and "description" in fm
   assert len(fm["description"]) <= 1024
   assert len(content) <= 100_000
   ```
5. **Git add + commit** on the active branch.
6. **Note:** the CURRENT session's skill loader is cached — `skill_view` / `skills_list` will not see the new skill until a new session. This is expected, not a bug.

## Cross-Referencing Other Skills

`metadata.hermes.related_skills` unions both trees (`skills/` in-repo and `~/.hermes/skills/`) at load time. You CAN reference a user-local skill from an in-repo skill, but it won't resolve for other users who clone the repo fresh. Prefer referencing only in-repo skills from in-repo skills. If a frequently-referenced skill lives only in `~/.hermes/skills/`, consider promoting it to the repo.

## Editing Existing In-Repo Skills

- **Small fix (typo, added pitfall, tightened trigger):** `skill_manage(action='patch', name=..., old_string=..., new_string=...)` works fine on in-repo skills.
- **Major rewrite:** `write_file` the whole SKILL.md. `skill_manage(action='edit')` also works but requires supplying the full new content.
- **Adding supporting files:** `write_file` to `skills/<category>/<name>/references/<file>.md`, `templates/<file>`, or `scripts/<file>`. `skill_manage(action='write_file')` also works and enforces the references/templates/scripts/assets subdir allowlist.
- **Always commit** the edit — in-repo skills are source, not runtime state.

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Using `skill_manage(action='create')` for an in-repo skill.** It writes to `~/.hermes/skills/`, not the repo tree. Use `write_file` for in-repo creation.

2. **Leading whitespace before `---`.** The validator checks `content.startswith("---")`; any leading blank line or BOM fails validation.

3. **Description too generic.** Peer descriptions start with "Use when ..." and describe the *trigger class*, not the one task. "Use when debugging X" > "Debug X".

4. **Forgetting the author/license/metadata block.** Not validator-enforced, but every peer has it; omitting makes the skill look half-finished.

5. **Writing a skill that duplicates a peer.** Before creating, `ls skills/<category>/` and open 2-3 peers. Prefer extending an existing skill to creating a narrow sibling.

6. **Expecting the current session to see the new skill.** It won't. The skill loader is initialized at session start. Verify in a fresh session or via `skill_view` using the exact path.

7. **Linking to skills that don't exist in-repo.** `related_skills: [some-user-local-skill]` works for you but breaks for other clones. Prefer only in-repo links.

## Verification Checklist

- [ ] File is at `skills/<category>/<name>/SKILL.md` (not in `~/.hermes/skills/`)
- [ ] Frontmatter starts at byte 0 with `---`, closes with `\n---\n`
- [ ] `name`, `description`, `version`, `author`, `license`, `metadata.hermes.{tags, related_skills}` all present
- [ ] Name ≤ 64 chars, lowercase + hyphens
- [ ] Description ≤ 1024 chars and starts with "Use when ..."
- [ ] Total file ≤ 100,000 chars (aim for 8-15k)
- [ ] Structure: `# Title` → `## Overview` → `## When to Use` → body → `## Common Pitfalls` → `## Verification Checklist`
- [ ] `related_skills` references resolve in-repo (or are explicitly OK to be user-local)
- [ ] `git add skills/<category>/<name>/ && git commit` completed on the intended branch

function.arguments:
{"action": "create", "category": "data-science", "content": "---
name: config-driven-etl
description: >-
 Build ETL scripts with config-driven architecture: YAML column mappings +
 stable Python engine + fuzzy column matching. When input format changes,
 edit the YAML, not the Python. Separation of concerns between two AI agents
 (config-maintainer vs logic-maintainer).
version: 1.0.0
author: Hermes Agent
license: MIT
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [etl, data-processing, excel, config-driven, fuzzy-matching]
    related_skills: []
---

# Config-Driven ETL (YAML + Python + Fuzzy Matching)

## Overview

When processing Excel reports whose column names change periodically (e.g.,
finance teams rename columns, add new sheets), hardcoded column mappings
break the pipeline. The config-driven pattern solves this by splitting the
system into three parts:

1. **schema_config.yaml** — column aliases, file patterns, sheet names,
   business rules, overrides. Editable by the config-maintainer AI.
2. **engine.py** — stable Python logic: reads YAML, applies fuzzy column
   matching, transforms data. Modified only for new business logic.
3. **Fuzzy column matching** — token-overlap (Jaccard similarity) algorithm
   that tolerates column renames, case changes, and minor variations.

**Separation of ownership:** A routine format change (column renamed) needs
only a YAML edit. A new business rule (new segment, new calculation) needs
a Python change.

## When to Use

- You process Excel/CSV reports whose column names change periodically
- Multiple AI agents maintain the system (one edits config, one edits logic)
- The input format is controlled by an external team (finance, ops, etc.)
- You want to reduce pipeline breakage from cosmetic column renames

**Don't use for:**
- Fixed-format data where columns never change
- Simple one-off transformations
- Real-time streaming data

## Architecture

```
project/
├── schema_config.yaml    # ← EDIT THIS when format changes
├── etl_engine.py         # ← Stable Python (config-driven)
├── SKILL.md              # ← instructions for both AIs
├── data/                 # output directory
└── source/               # input files
```

### YAML Config Structure

```yaml
# --- File discovery ---
file_patterns:
  source_a:
    - "*pattern_a*.xlsx"
    - "*alt_name*.xlsx"
  source_b:
    - "*pattern_b*.xlsx"

# --- Sheet names ---
sheet_names:
  source_b: "Custom Sheet Name"

# --- Column mappings (key = output field, value = [alias1, alias2, ...]) ---
column_mappings:
  customer_name:
    - "FINAL Ship to Name"
    - "Customer Name"
    - "Ship-to Name"
    - "Client"
  revenue_eur:
    - "EUR Veolia P&L"
    - "Revenue EUR"
    - "Revenus EUR"
    - "EUR"
    - "Amount"

# --- Output column names and order ---
output_columns:
  customer_name: "Customer"
  revenue_eur: "Revenue (EUR)"
output_column_order:
  - "Customer"
  - "Revenue (EUR)"

# --- Business rules ---
segment_overrides:
  - name_contains: "EXYTE"
    segment: "Microelectronics"
    ship_to_ids: ["4000237000"]

bu_normalization:
  default: "HQ"
  rules:
    - pattern: "EPP|ES PROJECT"
      replacement: "EPP"
```

### Fuzzy Column Matching Logic

The core technique is **token-overlap (Jaccard) similarity**:

```python
def _tokenize(s: str) -> set:
    return set(re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", s.lower()))

def match_score(col_name: str, alias: str) -> float:
    """Jaccard similarity of word tokens. 1.0 = exact match."""
    a = _tokenize(col_name)
    b = _tokenize(alias)
    if not a or not b:
        return 1.0 if col_name.lower() == alias.lower() else 0.0
    return len(a & b) / len(a | b)

def find_column(df, aliases: list[str], threshold: float = 0.6):
    """Find best-matching column. Exact match wins immediately."""
    best_score, best_col = 0.0, None
    for col in df.columns:
        col_str = str(col)
        for alias in aliases:
            if col_str.lower().strip() == alias.lower().strip():
                return col_str
            score = match_score(col_str, alias)
            if score > best_score:
                best_score, best_col = score, col_str
    return best_col if best_score >= threshold else None
```

Tune the `threshold` per dataset — 0.6 works well for most column names.

## Workflow

### Step 1: Audit current input files
```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_excel("path/to/file.xlsx", sheet_name="Sheet1")
print(list(df.columns))
```

### Step 2: Build column_mappings in schema_config.yaml
For each output field, list every known column name from every file version.
Order: most specific first, most generic last. Exact match wins immediately,
so put the current canonical name first.

### Step 3: Write stable Python engine
- Load YAML with `yaml.safe_load()`
- For each input source, call `find_column()` per output field
- Handle optional columns gracefully: `df.get(col, "")` or fill ""

### Step 4: Test with all known file versions
```bash
python etl_engine.py "source/old_format.xlsx" "source/old_format2.xlsx"
python etl_engine.py "source/new_format.xlsx" "source/new_format2.xlsx"
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Token overlap too low for short columns.** Columns like "ID", "EUR",
   "BU" have few tokens. Add explicit case-insensitive exact-match early
   exit in `find_column()` (the implementation above already does this).

2. **False positive matches.** `"FINAL Ship to Name"` and `"Ship-to Country"`
   share tokens `["ship", "to"]` → 50% overlap. Set threshold high enough
   (>0.5) and order aliases with distinguishing tokens first.

3. **Missing columns should warn, not crash.** Use `.get()` with defaults
   for optional fields. Log warnings for unexpected missing required fields.

4. **Multiple files matching the same pattern.** Use glob ordering + earliest
   match, or add a date-based tiebreaker.

5. **Forgetting to update the YAML when testing.** Make sure the engine
   reads the config file at runtime, not from a cached copy.

## References

See `references/fuzzy-column-matching.py` for a standalone implementation
with test cases. See `references/config-driven-etl-example.yaml` for a
complete real-world schema_config.yaml example.

## Verification Checklist

- [ ] schema_config.yaml has aliases for all known column names (old + new)
- [ ] Threshold is tuned (test with worst-case column rename)
- [ ] Optional columns produce "" instead of KeyError
- [ ] All input file versions produce consistent output
- [ ] Python engine has no hardcoded column names, file patterns, or sheet names
- [ ] Config file is documented so the config-maintainer AI knows what to edit", "name": "config-driven-etl"}

function.arguments:
{"action": "write_file", "file_content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Standalone fuzzy column matching — copy into your ETL engine.

Usage:
    python fuzzy-column-matching.py
    # Runs self-test with example columns
"""

import re
from typing import Optional


def _tokenize(s: str) -> set[str]:
    """Split a string into lowercase alphanumeric tokens."""
    return set(re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", s.lower()))


def match_score(col_name: str, alias: str) -> float:
    """
    Jaccard similarity of word tokens between two strings.
    
    Returns 0.0 (no overlap) to 1.0 (identical token sets or exact match).
    """
    a_tokens = _tokenize(col_name)
    b_tokens = _tokenize(alias)
    if not a_tokens or not b_tokens:
        # One or both strings have no alphanumeric tokens → exact char compare
        return 1.0 if col_name.lower() == alias.lower() else 0.0
    intersection = a_tokens & b_tokens
    union = a_tokens | b_tokens
    return len(intersection) / len(union)


def find_column(
    available_columns: list[str],
    aliases: list[str],
    threshold: float = 0.6,
) -> Optional[str]:
    """
    Find the best-matching column from available_columns given a list of aliases.
    
    - Exact case-insensitive match wins immediately (score = 1.0).
    - Otherwise, returns the column with highest Jaccard similarity ≥ threshold.
    - Returns None when no column meets the threshold.
    
    Example:
        cols = ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"]
        find_column(cols, ["Ship to Name", "Customer Name"])
        # → "FINAL Ship to Name"  (token overlap ~0.67)
    """
    best_score = 0.0
    best_col: Optional[str] = None
    
    for col in available_columns:
        col_str = str(col)
        col_lower = col_str.lower().strip()
        
        for alias in aliases:
            alias_lower = alias.lower().strip()
            
            # Exact match → immediate win
            if col_lower == alias_lower:
                return col_str
            
            # Fuzzy match
            score = match_score(col_str, alias)
            if score > best_score:
                best_score = score
                best_col = col_str
    
    return best_col if best_score >= threshold else None


# ── Self-test ──

def _test():
    tests = [
        # (available_columns, aliases, expected)
        (
            ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"],
            ["Ship to Name", "Customer Name"],
            "FINAL Ship to Name",
        ),
        (
            ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"],
            ["FINAL SIC CODE", "SIC Code", "Customer SIC"],
            "Customer SIC",
        ),
        (
            ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"],
            ["EUR Veolia P&L", "Revenue EUR", "EUR"],
            "EUR Veolia P&L",  # exact match
        ),
        (
            ["Managerial BU", "Customer Name", "Revenus EUR"],
            ["hierarchy_1", "BU Hierarchy", "Managerial BU"],
            "Managerial BU",  # exact match on second alias
        ),
        (
            ["Managerial BU", "Customer Name", "Revenus EUR"],
            ["EUR Veolia P&L", "Revenue EUR", "Revenus EUR"],
            "Revenus EUR",  # exact match
        ),
        (
            ["Unnamed: 15", "Unnamed: 16"],
            ["Revenue EUR", "EUR"],
            None,  # no match → None
        ),
        # Short column names (few tokens)
        (
            ["Year", "Month", "EUR"],
            ["Year of Posting Date", "Year"],
            "Year",  # exact match
        ),
        # Case variations
        (
            ["year of posting date", "month", "eur"],
            ["Year of Posting Date in the Document"],
            "year of posting date",  # exact (case-insensitive)
        ),
    ]
    
    all_pass = True
    for cols, aliases, expected in tests:
        result = find_column(cols, aliases)
        status = "✅" if result == expected else "❌"
        if result != expected:
            all_pass = False
            print(
                f"{status} find_column({cols}, {aliases})\n"
                f"   expected={expected!r}, got={result!r}"
            )
    
    if all_pass:
        print("✅ All fuzzy column matching tests passed.")
    else:
        print("❌ Some tests FAILED.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    _test()

 edit the YAML, not the Python. Separation of concerns between two AI agents
 (config-maintainer vs logic-maintainer).
version: 1.0.0

---

# Config-Driven ETL (YAML + Python + Fuzzy Matching)

## Overview

When processing Excel reports whose column names change periodically (e.g.,
finance teams rename columns, add new sheets), hardcoded column mappings
break the pipeline. The config-driven pattern solves this by splitting the
system into three parts:

1. **schema_config.yaml** — column aliases, file patterns, sheet names,
   business rules, overrides. Editable by the config-maintainer AI.
2. **engine.py** — stable Python logic: reads YAML, applies fuzzy column
   matching, transforms data. Modified only for new business logic.
3. **Fuzzy column matching** — token-overlap (Jaccard similarity) algorithm
   that tolerates column renames, case changes, and minor variations.

**Separation of ownership:** A routine format change (column renamed) needs
only a YAML edit. A new business rule (new segment, new calculation) needs
a Python change.

## When to Use

- You process Excel/CSV reports whose column names change periodically
- Multiple AI agents maintain the system (one edits config, one edits logic)
- The input format is controlled by an external team (finance, ops, etc.)
- You want to reduce pipeline breakage from cosmetic column renames

**Don't use for:**
- Fixed-format data where columns never change
- Simple one-off transformations
- Real-time streaming data

## Architecture

```
project/
├── schema_config.yaml    # ← EDIT THIS when format changes
├── etl_engine.py         # ← Stable Python (config-driven)
├── SKILL.md              # ← instructions for both AIs
├── data/                 # output directory
└── source/               # input files
```

### YAML Config Structure

```yaml
# --- File discovery ---
file_patterns:
  source_a:
    - "*pattern_a*.xlsx"
    - "*alt_name*.xlsx"
  source_b:
    - "*pattern_b*.xlsx"

# --- Sheet names ---
sheet_names:
  source_b: "Custom Sheet Name"

# --- Column mappings (key = output field, value = [alias1, alias2, ...]) ---
column_mappings:
  customer_name:
    - "FINAL Ship to Name"
    - "Customer Name"
    - "Ship-to Name"
    - "Client"
  revenue_eur:
    - "EUR Veolia P&L"
    - "Revenue EUR"
    - "Revenus EUR"
    - "EUR"
    - "Amount"

# --- Output column names and order ---
output_columns:
  customer_name: "Customer"
  revenue_eur: "Revenue (EUR)"
output_column_order:
  - "Customer"
  - "Revenue (EUR)"

# --- Business rules ---
segment_overrides:
  - name_contains: "EXYTE"
    segment: "Microelectronics"
    ship_to_ids: ["4000237000"]

bu_normalization:
  default: "HQ"
  rules:
    - pattern: "EPP|ES PROJECT"
      replacement: "EPP"
```

### Fuzzy Column Matching Logic

The core technique is **token-overlap (Jaccard) similarity**:

```python
def _tokenize(s: str) -> set:
    return set(re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", s.lower()))

def match_score(col_name: str, alias: str) -> float:
    """Jaccard similarity of word tokens. 1.0 = exact match."""
    a = _tokenize(col_name)
    b = _tokenize(alias)
    if not a or not b:
        return 1.0 if col_name.lower() == alias.lower() else 0.0
    return len(a & b) / len(a | b)

def find_column(df, aliases: list[str], threshold: float = 0.6):
    """Find best-matching column. Exact match wins immediately."""
    best_score, best_col = 0.0, None
    for col in df.columns:
        col_str = str(col)
        for alias in aliases:
            if col_str.lower().strip() == alias.lower().strip():
                return col_str
            score = match_score(col_str, alias)
            if score > best_score:
                best_score, best_col = score, col_str
    return best_col if best_score >= threshold else None
```

Tune the `threshold` per dataset — 0.6 works well for most column names.

## Workflow

### Step 1: Audit current input files
```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_excel("path/to/file.xlsx", sheet_name="Sheet1")
print(list(df.columns))
```

### Step 2: Build column_mappings in schema_config.yaml
For each output field, list every known column name from every file version.
Order: most specific first, most generic last. Exact match wins immediately,
so put the current canonical name first.

### Step 3: Write stable Python engine
- Load YAML with `yaml.safe_load()`
- For each input source, call `find_column()` per output field
- Handle optional columns gracefully: `df.get(col, "")` or fill ""

### Step 4: Test with all known file versions
```bash
python etl_engine.py "source/old_format.xlsx" "source/old_format2.xlsx"
python etl_engine.py "source/new_format.xlsx" "source/new_format2.xlsx"
```

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Token overlap too low for short columns.** Columns like "ID", "EUR",
   "BU" have few tokens. Add explicit case-insensitive exact-match early
   exit in `find_column()` (the implementation above already does this).

2. **False positive matches.** `"FINAL Ship to Name"` and `"Ship-to Country"`
   share tokens `["ship", "to"]` → 50% overlap. Set threshold high enough
   (>0.5) and order aliases with distinguishing tokens first.

3. **Missing columns should warn, not crash.** Use `.get()` with defaults
   for optional fields. Log warnings for unexpected missing required fields.

4. **Multiple files matching the same pattern.** Use glob ordering + earliest
   match, or add a date-based tiebreaker.

5. **Forgetting to update the YAML when testing.** Make sure the engine
   reads the config file at runtime, not from a cached copy.

## References

See `references/fuzzy-column-matching.py` for a standalone implementation
with test cases. See `references/config-driven-etl-example.yaml` for a
complete real-world schema_config.yaml example.

## Verification Checklist

- [ ] schema_config.yaml has aliases for all known column names (old + new)
- [ ] Threshold is tuned (test with worst-case column rename)
- [ ] Optional columns produce "" instead of KeyError
- [ ] All input file versions produce consistent output
- [ ] Python engine has no hardcoded column names, file patterns, or sheet names
- [ ] Config file is documented so the config-maintainer AI knows what to edit", "path": "data-science/config-driven-etl/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/home/alfdib/hermes/skills/data-science/config-driven-etl", "linked_files": null, "usage_hint": null, "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available", "metadata": {"hermes": {"tags": ["etl", "data-processing", "excel", "config-driven", "fuzzy-matching"], "related_skills": []}}}

{"success": true, "name": "config-driven-etl", "file": "references/fuzzy-column-matching.py", "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Standalone fuzzy column matching — copy into your ETL engine.

Usage:
    python fuzzy-column-matching.py
    # Runs self-test with example columns
"""

import re
from typing import Optional


def _tokenize(s: str) -> set[str]:
    """Split a string into lowercase alphanumeric tokens."""
    return set(re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", s.lower()))


def match_score(col_name: str, alias: str) -> float:
    """
    Jaccard similarity of word tokens between two strings.
    
    Returns 0.0 (no overlap) to 1.0 (identical token sets or exact match).
    """
    a_tokens = _tokenize(col_name)
    b_tokens = _tokenize(alias)
    if not a_tokens or not b_tokens:
        # One or both strings have no alphanumeric tokens → exact char compare
        return 1.0 if col_name.lower() == alias.lower() else 0.0
    intersection = a_tokens & b_tokens
    union = a_tokens | b_tokens
    return len(intersection) / len(union)


def find_column(
    available_columns: list[str],
    aliases: list[str],
    threshold: float = 0.6,
) -> Optional[str]:
    """
    Find the best-matching column from available_columns given a list of aliases.
    
    - Exact case-insensitive match wins immediately (score = 1.0).
    - Otherwise, returns the column with highest Jaccard similarity ≥ threshold.
    - Returns None when no column meets the threshold.
    
    Example:
        cols = ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"]
        find_column(cols, ["Ship to Name", "Customer Name"])
        # → "FINAL Ship to Name"  (token overlap ~0.67)
    """
    best_score = 0.0
    best_col: Optional[str] = None
    
    for col in available_columns:
        col_str = str(col)
        col_lower = col_str.lower().strip()
        
        for alias in aliases:
            alias_lower = alias.lower().strip()
            
            # Exact match → immediate win
            if col_lower == alias_lower:
                return col_str
            
            # Fuzzy match
            score = match_score(col_str, alias)
            if score > best_score:
                best_score = score
                best_col = col_str
    
    return best_col if best_score >= threshold else None


# ── Self-test ──

def _test():
    tests = [
        # (available_columns, aliases, expected)
        (
            ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"],
            ["Ship to Name", "Customer Name"],
            "FINAL Ship to Name",
        ),
        (
            ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"],
            ["FINAL SIC CODE", "SIC Code", "Customer SIC"],
            "Customer SIC",
        ),
        (
            ["FINAL Ship to Name", "Customer SIC", "EUR Veolia P&L"],
            ["EUR Veolia P&L", "Revenue EUR", "EUR"],
            "EUR Veolia P&L",  # exact match
        ),
        (
            ["Managerial BU", "Customer Name", "Revenus EUR"],
            ["hierarchy_1", "BU Hierarchy", "Managerial BU"],
            "Managerial BU",  # exact match on second alias
        ),
        (
            ["Managerial BU", "Customer Name", "Revenus EUR"],
            ["EUR Veolia P&L", "Revenue EUR", "Revenus EUR"],
            "Revenus EUR",  # exact match
        ),
        (
            ["Unnamed: 15", "Unnamed: 16"],
            ["Revenue EUR", "EUR"],
            None,  # no match → None
        ),
        # Short column names (few tokens)
        (
            ["Year", "Month", "EUR"],
            ["Year of Posting Date", "Year"],
            "Year",  # exact match
        ),
        # Case variations
        (
            ["year of posting date", "month", "eur"],
            ["Year of Posting Date in the Document"],
            "year of posting date",  # exact (case-insensitive)
        ),
    ]
    
    all_pass = True
    for cols, aliases, expected in tests:
        result = find_column(cols, aliases)
        status = "✅" if result == expected else "❌"
        if result != expected:
            all_pass = False
            print(
                f"{status} find_column({cols}, {aliases})\n"
                f"   expected={expected!r}, got={result!r}"
            )
    
    if all_pass:
        print("✅ All fuzzy column matching tests passed.")
    else:
        print("❌ Some tests FAILED.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    _test()
